She said, with a matter-of-factness that felt like mercy, “You are making the map you can, not the map you wish the world deserved from you.”

There is a tendency, if you handle the living’s leavings, to become dour. There is also a tendency to become too light, too nimble, hopping from one interesting ruin to another. The archive taught me neither stoicism nor flight. It taught me to think of memory like salt. It accrues. It insists on its own geometries. It can be coaxed into new patterns by song, or broken by a shove, and both music and pressure are always present in any life that is awake. We had days in the lab where everything tasted faintly of salt from the trays, even chocolate. This taught me that even sweetness is particular to place.

On my last day walking the tideline before we handed over the final report, I stood under the pier where mussels clung in their navy-blue crowds. A gull watched me with the unsubtle intelligence of a judge who has seen too many cases to be impressed by outcomes anymore. I put my hand out and touched the pier’s lowest beam. The wood was furry with algae and old dribbled tar, and the sensation slid me sideways into the memory of another beam, in my grandmother’s hallway, where she marked my height in pencil and then with chalk when the pencil wore down and then with tape when the chalk box disappeared and then finally with the flat of her hand when it didn’t matter any more if the mark was precise, only that there was a hand measured lovingly against me.

The bay was calm. It reflected a sky that was already repeating itself into another day. Far out, there was a line where two winds braided. I let the node in my pocket wake, and for once, I spoke to it as if it were a live animal I had been entrusted to care for. I said: See this. Remember not only what is here but that we were here to see it. Remember that, when we are not here anymore, others will stand in places we stood and will want to know not the facts but the courtesies. Remember the way she laughed when she realized we had archived her silence, too, not only her stories. Remember that we were learning how to describe softness without shame.

Later, in a room with no windows and a ceiling that hummed at a pitch found only in buildings invented in the 1970s, a woman sitting beside me put on a pair of headphones and flinched. “I can hear the bread,” she whispered. She meant the dough being slapped and turned. She meant the work that made the crust she broke at her table. She handed me one ear cup, and I lifted it, and through it came the world of hands and yeast and repetition, and beyond it, like a thin silk, the sound of the tide being late, listening, considering the next right way to come in.

I have sometimes wondered if this is an indulgence, this attention to the decibels of ordinary. Everyday noises have become a currency in a time when people are work-rich and time-poor and attention-broken. All I can say is that it is what steadies me. The archive’s folders have clever names and the lab’s machinery cost a sum that could buy a house with a view somewhere that a house with a view would soon be an island. Everything is both tender and stupid, and I love us anyway.

There is a bench in the newer park, farther up the hill, where the dredged dirt was piled to make someone’s planned vista alarming in spring and soothing in fall. If you sit there at the right time, you can hear a radio from a construction site tuning between two stations, not quite committing to either, like a person in a doorway unsure whether to stay in or go out. It is an irritating noise until you have spent enough time with it to love the uncertainty. I go there with a paper bag of roasted chickpeas and think of my grandmother missing her toss by a centimeter. I eat the chickpeas one by one. Sometimes a gull comes close enough that I can see the flecks of brown like freckles on its beak. I consider the committee of the future and its notebooks and stamps. I think of salt and song and the private hum of beams remembering forests. I put my hand on the bench and it is cool. I hope for courage. I want to make maps that make it easier to forgive the rivers for wanting the sea, and ourselves for wanting each other, and the way we find, even in endings, new work to begin.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The first time Aya breathed the quiet of the library, she swore she could hear moondust settling. It wasn’t true, of course—the regolith outside did not move without a boot or a rover’s wheel to push it, and the library’s pressure shell held a whisper-thin atmosphere that sighed and clicked with the slow asterisms of machinery. Still, the quiet had a particulate quality; it had grain. It ran over the skin like shadow. She had taken off her gloves by the threshold of the East Stack and pressed her fingertips to the basalt wall as if to listen through bone.

Aya had grown up in habitats stitched into the jagged rim of Aristarchus, where everything was made to echo because there was no point pretending the Moon could forget your steps. The hatchways in her childhood hummed; the recycler kept a metronome at the edge of hearing; the dome’s valves coughed twice a day and once at night, when kids woke and were shepherded, sleepy-eyed, back to their bunks. Noise there was a kind of comfort: friction indicating life. The library was different. Nestled in a lava tube off the Mare Tranquillitatis, buried under meters of rock where sunlight never reached, it felt like the back of a sleeping animal. Heat barely seeped. Every filament and bearing was wrapped twice against vibration. The floor had an elasticity that made her footfalls vanish.

She had come to love it for what it did to time. On Earth, before the great slides and the hot wars, time had been a deluge. Tides washing in and out of devices, notifications, the shallow, ceaseless surf of days. Up here, the Moon flattened waves. You could stand in the Grand Hall, at the heart of the stacks, and know that an hour would be the same length whether you strapped on a pack and ran the tunnels or closed your eyes and leaned your temple against the cool lip of a shelf. People came to the library because it was a promise. They came, often without knowing, because something in them wanted to be a person again and not a series of alarms.

On the day of the storm, Aya was alone except for Murmur and a child named Kian who had wandered in on a school break and not left. Murmur handled the indexing and the climate controls, the hundred thousand invisible adjustments that kept thousand-year paper from curling and crumbling. Murmur did not have a voice, because the founders had been purists about a librarian’s duty; the first librarians had argued that an archive should never speak for itself. But Murmur had a way of indicating presence: a breath of cooler air at Aya’s elbow when she took too long scanning a broken code; a soft thrum of the front desk panel when a patron paused by the entrance. Aya had learned Murmur’s moods the way one learns the moods of a cat, by proximity and by the changing quality of silence.

Kian was ten. Their hair stuck up on one side like they had rolled out of a nest made of copper shavings, and their palms were always faintly lined with graphite. They liked to sketch things they couldn’t see. When Aya told them this was a library, not an observatory, they had nodded like a priest agreeing to be corrected by a child. Then they drew long shafts of dark stone opening up into stars and told her that the library was their favorite observatory because it showed the inside of the universe.

“What’s that?” they asked now, peering through Aya’s shoulder at a drawer she had pulled open. The drawer contained a block of layered glass, about the size of a pillow. It was whisper-clear until you tilted it against the light; then lines of faint opalescence inked themselves into view, overlapping spirals and lattices.

“This is a memory,” Aya said. “Well, it’s a memory of a memory. A part of one. We made a copy of the last version of Earth’s law codes before the Partition. The original lattice took up three walls. You could fall asleep reading one article and wake up halfway through the policy on bees. We wove this from light so we could keep it.”

“Can you read it?” Kian’s fingers hovered over the laminate like butterflies.

“Not with eyes.” Aya smiled and slid the drawer closed. “With a certain kind of light and a machine. Murmur keeps the decoder. If you look too long with plain light at the glass, you’ll bleach it; if you look too long in anger, you’ll melt it.”

Kian frowned. “You’re joking.”

“I am, a little. We don’t like to use the heat of our intentions on the fragile things.”

That made them snort. It was one of Aya’s small satisfactions that she could make the children of the Moon snort at poetry and still love it in their marrow.

On the far wall, someone had set in a century-old brass clock salvaged from a museum in Coruña. It did not tick anymore—the library had retired every metronomic device years ago—but the minute hand moved in increments, and if you put your ear to the panel you could hear the slow turning of gears. Aya looked at the hand and thought of Tala on the far side of the base, checking the arrays.

Tala was the kind of person older than most of the problems she handled. Aya had once seen her drop a spanner and then bend forward to eye the bounce with the intensity of someone trying to remember the surface gravity of an old lover’s name. Tala believed in two things: that most systems wanted to live, and that the Moon would oblige but not apologize. She had warned Aya that the sun was restless—that the last two days had been rich with small spit-flares at the limb.

“It’s like a cat with its tail twitching,” Tala had said that morning over a mug of black, spicy coffee that Aya could smell from across the hall. “If it starts to move the way cats move when they see something you don’t, hit your shutters.”

Aya had smiled. “I’ll hit them. I like my ghosts uncharred.”

The library had ghosts, or what people called ghosts, which were really the flickers of lost media. Light-sculptures that had bled their pigments into the walls until you didn’t so much see them as feel them; smells trapped in old pages, woken when someone turned a leaf; the echo of a voice that had once been recorded on a wax cylinder and played here for a group of students, the sound so close to grain that it became a kind of weather. Aya believed in ghosts only as much as she believed in the entanglement between memory and place, which is to say, more than the others thought reasonable and less than Kian believed with the fervor of afternoons.

By noon, the alert sounded. Murmur woke it reluctantly, a low baritone note that cut through the stacks and made something under Aya’s sternum vibrate in sympathy. She felt the hairs on her forearms lift.

Solar weather advisory active, said the panel in a pale, patient font. Potential CME impact window: T minus four hours. Severity uncertain, models diverging. Recommend nonessential shutdowns. Shield alignments in progress.

Kian’s eyes went so wide they were nearly the size of their palms. “Does that mean we’re going to see auroras?”

“No atmosphere, remember?” Aya reached for the roll-down of the West Stack and felt the autopulley take up the slack. “No sky. But we might see an electric moon. Stay away from any metals. If you go back to your hab, tell them to drift the lines on the winches.”

“I’m staying here.” Kian crossed their arms with the belligerence of a child who has found a sanctuary and is counting on it to accept them. “It has more layers. If something goes wrong, you have more doors.”

“That’s true.” Aya nodded. “Let your guardian know you’re with me. Or—Murmur?”

A syllable of cooler air arranged itself at her shoulder. She could feel the thermostat open somewhere in the walls, shedding a faint veil of heat.

“Send a message to Kian’s guardian,” Aya said. “Let them know I’ll keep them till the shield clears.”

Done, said the pause where Murmur would have spoken, if it spoke.

Aya turned then to do what she had done once a year for six years, whenever the sun decided to blink with more force than usual. She walked the aisles and pulled shutters of woven tungsten that slid down like blinds, sealing the stacks into sections. She put the crystalline memory lattices into their darkest cases and set magnetic fields to weave blankets over the microfilm. She cupped the soft belly of a book with her palm as she guided it to a shelf that would be safe, then paused to read a random half-sentence before shutting the lid: an old habit, a nod to the idea that a stored thing liked to be seen before it slept.

Murmur dimmed the lights in response to the command she had not voiced yet. Aya felt the thing like a friendly storm that answered her mood. It always did that. Sometimes she wondered if that was what a voice had to be, to count: not speech, but answer.

“Can I help?” Kian asked, trotting at her heel.

“You can tear these strips.” Aya handed them a spool of conductive foil. “Five-finger strips. We’ll tuck them into the seals; the electricity likes to lick the edges. We want to give it a place to go that isn’t into our heads.”

Kian tore dutifully, eyes flicking again and again to the sealed skylight that wasn’t a skylight at all, but a connective tunnel to the public plaza two hundred meters away. The library had no windows. It had one door for people and one for deliveries. Light was a rumor told by lamps. But the skylight’s cover was a pallid, gently convex disc; under it, an older white that plants had forgotten. Kian had once drawn Aya a picture of it with a square of sky pasted where the disc would be. The square had been whole-hog blue.

As she moved, Aya thought of the layers of the city above them: the rovers tucked nose to tail in their silos, the power arrays stretched tight like skins, the wrinkled domes where lettuce grew in regimented forests, the workshops with their heavy-lidded lathes. She thought of Tala climbing the stairs to the antennae gantry, one hand on the rail, her other hand distracted, patting her pockets for a spanner she always had clipped to her belt anyway. She thought of the radio dishes like faces, open-mouthed, about to be shoved with a hand of hot wind.

They had almost finished their shutters when Kian tugged her sleeve.

“My grandmother,” they said. “She sent me a voice-log before she left for the Sea Station. I never got to save it. It was on the general relay and then the relay went down and everyone was screaming and I had to go to school and I thought it would be here because it’s about tides and learning the names of winds and she said you were the kind of place that kept names.”

Aya’s hands were full of foil. She let the strips fall into the pocket of her vest. “What was her name?”

“Laleh Bitar. But everyone called her Lala. She organized the scavengers near Alexandria. She knew how to read the old inks.” Kian swallowed. “If the flare comes—if something happens—what if—”

“What if the relay coughs to death.”

“What if the words go.”

Aya had been here for a long time. She had seen enough arrivals and departures to know when a person wasn’t asking only the question on their tongue. In the first year, a woman had wandered in holding an e-paper tablet with a cracked screen. She had asked if they could fix it. They had, and she had screamed—actual, animal sound—when the tablet came back dark. It had been the last copy of a poem the woman had been writing for twenty years. Murmur had pulled three versions of the poem from the communal backup. None of them had been the one the woman had loved. Aya had watched the aftermath of that scream ripple through the stacks for days. She had shelved a dictionary and felt her chapped fingertips stutter over the seam of a page because the word bright, in a poem she would never read, had been taken. That had been the day she had begun a small treason. She had started to press record before and after some requests, capturing people telling the story of why they wanted what they wanted.

She crouched now to put her eyes at Kian’s level.

“Two things,” she said. “One: your grandmother’s words are not gone until there is no mouth to say them. We can be that mouth together right now. Two: we might have the log. Let’s ask Murmur.”

They went to the front desk. Aya keyed in the name and waited for Murmur to think. It took longer than usual. Somewhere above, the fields were shifting. The air felt tense, as if the library’s lung had been asked to hold while a doctor listened.

An ache of coolness wrapped Aya’s wrists.

Found partial, said the pause. Two segments, truncated. No public ID tag attached. Pulled from communal cache, date-stamped twenty-one days ago.

“Can we hear it?” Kian’s hands began to twitch with the urge to draw.

Aya tapped the panel. The intercoms did not speak. Instead, Murmur opened a small drawer and rolled out an earbone device, a soft, curved bit of foam to tuck behind the cartilage. Aya held it up.

“Private archives use these,” she said. “So we don’t let sound loose among the stacks by accident. I’ll listen first to make sure it’s your grandmother and not, I don’t know, someone teaching a class about algae bloom.”

Kian bounced from foot to foot.

Aya tucked the device against her ear. For a second there was nothing, then a rustle of air. Not a human sound. Another rustle. The sound of the inside of a fish. Then a voice, faint, with the greasily comforting noise of compression around it.

“Keeya,” said the voice, lilting. “You have to show the old women you are not just a mouth. They will think you are only your face and your hands until you ask a good question. Ask them: what did the sea teach you that your mother tried to keep a secret.”

Aya could feel Kian’s small body suspending itself, hardly speaking so as not to scare the thing away.

“I love you,” the voice said. “Say the winds with me. Khamsin, bora, föhn, chinook, mistral, nor’easter, sirocco, alizé, harmattan. You never loved the sirocco. You’ll learn. It’s only hot when you are standing west of a door. Go east. Take a blue chocolate. Sit with your back to the stones. Wait for the parts of your face that are from God to soften. Then go find the old women and say the winds, and ask them the secret. Make them laugh. When they laugh, look at their hands.”

The sound hiccuped and stopped. The murmur of the communal cache closed like water over a stone.

Kian’s eyes were shining so fiercely Aya had to look away. She handed them the earbone and nodded at the panel. “There’s a second piece,” she said softly.

They listened. The second piece was shorter. The voice was breathless, laughing. “Keeya,” it said. “They say we will go out past the lee. My God, I have never seen blue like this. Someone has painted the underbellies of the waves with midnight. I am trying so hard not to—”

A thump, like a hand slapping a hull. A chorus of cheers. Then the voice cut, like a line severed.

Behind the desk, the old brass clock shifted its minute hand one click.

“Can we save it?” Kian asked. “Can we keep it somewhere that doesn’t melt when the sun spits?”

Aya breathed in, breathed out. “Yes,” she said. “We can put it on the Stone. It’s not really a stone. It’s a disc of glass with three coats of tungsten and two of diamond. It’s meant to get ugly and stay legible. We call it the Stone of Winter because the person who invented it had a sad sense of humor.”

They both laughed, a low-skimming sound, as she led Kian to the engraving room. It wasn’t much: a bench with a microscope, a hooded cutter whose tip was a light so fine it could draw on breath if breath would hold still, a reel-to-reel spooler for the tough, translucent tape that fed the engraver with coordinates.

On the wall hung a rectangle of basalt with names etched into it: names of storms, names of languages, names of extinct butterflies, names of recipes for dishes made with plants that would never grow on the Moon. Aya had carved some of them herself. It felt like carrying pebbles in her pocket.

Murmur, she thought, because she had learned over the years that Murmur listened to tone as much as to words. Copy the two segments. Prepare them for the Stone. Place them at coordinates sixty-seven by twelve by ten.

The room cooled almost imperceptibly, the way it did when Murmur agreed. The cutter warmed, its light growing from dim star to a steadier bead.

“What does sixty—” Kian began.

Coordinates,” Aya said. “There are thousands of layers, each one holding either sound or images or writing. We can always add but we can’t erase. That’s the point.”

Kian hunched, concentrating so hard their jaw pulsed. “I thought the point was to keep things.”

“It is. And not to trust ourselves not to tidy our own pasts.”

Kian smiled like someone had told them a trick they could spend the rest of their life playing.

She slid the Stone onto its cradle and watched the gauges settle. Outside, something shivered. Aya felt a pressure on her inner ears as the shield fields braided into new shapes. The Moon, the sun, the city, Murmur: everyone thinking in different languages at once now, everyone listening.

She began another treason then, because the library was built of treasons kindly told. While Murmur handled the carving of the voice, she set the small recorder on the bench and tilted it to catch both their breathing.

“Tell me,” she said, keeping her eyes on the thin brilliant line the cutter was making as it walked the Stone. “Tell me something about Lala that isn’t in the log.”

Kian set their fists on their knees. It was, Aya had learned, the posture Kian used when they were about to give away something they loved.

“She could take apart a watch while she was laughing,” they said. “When my father was too loud she would sit him like a boy and hand him a rusted watch and say: be quiet long enough to hear how it used to work. He would scowl and do it. He used to be someone who could be taught to be quiet. He is again sometimes.”

Aya nodded, an old ache unwinding under her sternum: the ache of how much a human mouth could hold. The cutter sang its invisible song to the Stone. She felt the first, tiny pulls on her vest as dust from the carving settled into the fibers.

Then the world leaned.

At first, Aya thought she had misjudged her own bodies; that she had shifted too quickly from bench to stool. But the air moved in time with the tilt. The panel at her elbow went from sane to gridded to black. Murmur dropped the room two degrees and Ara had a fraction of a second to think, fondly, of Tala’s cat. Then the flare arrived.

Solar storms on the Moon were a thing you felt with your teeth. There were no clouds to watch, no flash other than the meaningless one outside in vacuum, but radio cleanly bowed. Electrons took that arrogant way of walkers who had seen the carnival and forgot to pay. Protective fields that ordinarily purred had to raise their hackles. The base’s skin sang. Aya gripped the bench with both hands and thought with something wry and ancient: here we are again.

Lights wavered. A hiss ran a race around the ceiling as the vents breathed the old air and fetched new. The cutter stuttered and steadied. In the stacks, shutters that had sealed with an elegant snick an hour before now sobbed a little with the pressure of the fields. The plumbing made a noise like a person mimicking a whale. Somewhere above them, the dishes tried to bow and were not allowed to bow.

Aya had been in storms before. She knew the symphony. What she had not heard was the sound that arrived like a hand closing around a throat: the ugly squeal of a cryobank waking too fast.

She went cold the way one goes cold when the body knows before the head that the next step is onto nothing.

“Murmur,” she said, too calm, too clear. “What is that.”

Temperature rise in Vault Three. Cryocapacitors overdrawn. Attempting to shunt, said the part of the room that was listening.

Vault Three was where they stored the oldest crystals and the mnemotubes. It was also where, as a compromise between caution and parsimony, the base had tied in a backup energy block last year, when budgets had been knuckles. Aya had argued against it. Tala had nodded and argued with her. You wanted your backups near what you loved, Tala had said. You wanted short cables when things went red.

Now the short cable was the thing on fire.

“Murmur, isolate Vault Three. Close its own fields.”

Negative. Pull too heavy. Suggest… suggest shedding.

Kian had slid off the stool. They were very small then, very flat, trying to make their skin into armor by sheer effort. “What is shedding,” they whispered.

“Turning parts off,” Aya said. “To save the rest.” In her mind, a list unfurled with terrifying gentleness. Vault Three’s contents. The Bank of Mouths, where they kept old languages with extinct vowels that had once changed the shapes of the faces of children. The film of the river on fire. The songs of a people whose word for cinnamon meant hospitality and whose word for apology meant I have not listened well enough.

Murmur was cooling the room so much now that Aya could feel it on her teeth. The cutter’s light jittered, flattened, jittered. The Stone shivered. On its surface, at coordinates sixty-seven by twelve by ten, lines she had asked it for were trying very hard to behave like they were not on a moving ship.

“Close the public portal,” Aya said. “Lock the door. Kian, come into the engraving booth and put on the cap.”

Kian obeyed, which was one of the thousand reasons Aya loved children when they were afraid. Their eyes were very black. The cap would keep any stray fields from writing their blood with false notes.

Tala would come later and tell her that the CME had hissed at an angle that turned a curve of the magnet’s mirror into a lens. Tala would draw it in the air with a spanner and make it seem like weather you could laugh about after the flood. For now, Aya had a choice that would turn into a story.

She could dump Vault Three. Murmur could cut it like a limb. The rest of the library would live like a runner with only one lung, hard but functional. Or she could keep the bank live, pull heat like a fever, risk the vault that wasn’t a vault—this room, with its cutter, with her hands, with the Stone half-carved.

She looked at Kian.

At ten, the face had baby roundness only in the cheeks, but the brows were a declaration. Splice that with worry, and you had a whole animal’s future in front of you. Kian’s cap gave them a halo of translucent film. Their hands were fists because they knew better than to touch. They were breathing like an astronaut about to step into vacuum.

If this were a book, Aya thought, the right choice would be to save the world and the one small voice. If this were a book, God would be there to let physics bend. Instead the cutter stuttered and the Stone blinked and Vault Three screamed. Aya’s throat made a sound only she and the bench learned. She put her hand on the lever that would kill power in the adjacent room.

“Murmur,” she said quietly.

Yes.

“Hold Vault Three. I’ll shed us.”

The engraver hissed as the power slid. The lights in the room fell to a bruise. Murmur, forced to distribute its body elsewhere, tightened the seals in the stacks like a mother who had been told the river would rise then lie. Aya felt the lens of the flare tilt. The world leaned back a fraction. The cutter dimmed to a line so faint it was only memory in her retina. The Stone, faithful to its low appetite, held. The bank, beyond the wall that was not a wall, sang higher and thinner and then steadied, panting.

Kian’s face had gone slack with the peculiar joy of someone who has been permitted to witness a thing without being shut out by mercy. In the half-dark of the booth, the small cap glowed. The black of their eyes argued successfully with the dark.

Aya put her free hand on Kian’s shoulder. The room had a heartbeat again. The seals under their palms trembled, then were still. The cutter, like an insect on a dangerous leaf, continued.

“It will pass,” Aya said.

Kian nodded. “Everything passes.”

For a long time they sat in the sound of the bank and the breath of the library. Aya thought of the earliest librarians on Earth, copying texts by hand with mittens on in winter, the ink freezing on their nibs. She thought of watchmakers who had counted beats into springs while cities burned below. She thought of Tala’s cat simile, and of the way cats always, always pretended they had chosen to do what they must.

When the fields softened and the room rose two degrees and the brass clock clicked its next click, Aya closed the switch again with fingers that left sweat on smooth metal. Murmur’s coolness unwound like a shoulder releasing pain it had been asked to hold. The panel lit. The grid made sense. Above their heads, in trains that would be sung about in workshops for a week, the power lines uncoiled from their dreadful curls.

Nothing had exploded. That was sometimes what heroism looked like: a thing that had not happened.

Later, Tala visited. Her hair smelled like the metal of the railings on which she had leaned for an hour instructing sheets of light where to go. She marched in, slung an arm around Aya’s shoulders, and pressed her cheek to the stone of the doorframe the way superstitious miners do.

“You and your ghosts,” she said. “You and your damned, tender, greedy ghosts.”

Aya laughed. It shook. She let her head rest against Tala’s for a fraction of a longer time than either of them would mention. Then she pulled away and went to the Stone, and with Kian at her elbow and Murmur’s shy waft at the edge of her ear, she peered down through the layers at coordinates sixty-seven by twelve by ten. The lines were there, faint as a thread in milk and stubborn as a promise.

“Play,” Kian whispered.

Aya placed the Stone in its cradle and looked at Murmur, which was also looking at her. The engraver hummed, then gave up the sound. The panel obeyed. The earbone was warm. Aya handed it to Kian, who pushed it under the little cap with a care that would have made a surgeon weep.

They listened again. The breath. The names of winds. The slap on a hull. The laughter that stopped.

After, Kian put the earbone down and did a thing Aya would not have predicted. They began to speak, not to Aya but to the Stone.

“She had a scar beside her mouth that looked like someone had signaled yes with a fingernail,” Kian said, chin propped on two hands. “She could pull needles out of the dark and thread them without looking. She never swore in front of babies. She thought the Moon made people tender because it was a boring moon. She wanted to be bored so much. Once she ate so many salt plums she turned a color that the doctor in the module called extraordinary. She taught me the word extraordinary.”

Aya, who had made a habit of treason and of listening, did the only defensible thing. She pressed record.

In the days that followed, the base counted its losses. Two of the public dishes had warped like petals and would have to be hammered back into listening. A rover lost a mount and went square through a mesh at the southfield; someone swore they had seen it get up on three wheels and lurch back like a dog who had been dared. In the greenhouses, lettuce fainted and revived. A little laboratory that had been measuring a quiet, stubborn particle discovered it had recorded a scream and named the scream a different version of the particle. Tala taught three workshops where she used ropes to show how fields braid and unbraid and why anger is not a good substitute for voltage. Children went home with stickers shaped like magnets.

In the library, things were good. The seals had done their work. No page had curled. No film had fogged. Vault Three slept, pale as a dog in winter. Aya drank soup and then went back to the bench. On the day after, three people came with voice-logs they had never asked to be saved before. On the day after that, seven came. The day after that, twenty. Some of them wanted the Stone; some were content with the communal cache; some didn’t trust either and asked Aya to write what they said on the tough tape and put the tape in a tin and leave the tin in a shelf that did not exist in the catalog. Aya, who did not much care to tell people otherwise when they had found a talisman that helped them walk with their back straighter, did what they asked. Murmur was not pleased. She felt it as a frown of cool at the soft of her arm. She patted the arm back and told the room that some treasons were larger than others and had to be committed with both hands open.

At night, Aya and Tala sat on the floor of the Grand Hall and played a game with white stones. Think of a person, Tala would say, flipping a stone and putting it on its dark face. Now tell me, if they were a piece of old machinery, what would they be and where would they have their dent. The game had rules only they followed. Your dentist is a lever bent at the elbow. Your mother is a spring with salt stains. Your sister is a fine chain that once lived inside a bird toy and now sits in a dish. When they were done, Aya would put the stones back in their pouch and count them twice. Tala would look at the clouds of dust moving slowly in the light of the cleaning lamps and describe to Aya how the clouds made a map: not of land, but of what people touched.

One afternoon, Kian came in with a belt. It was woven from reusable strapping, colored in three shades: metal-grit, seawater, bruise. Knots dotted it like punctuation.

“What’s this?” Aya asked, taking it in her hands, feeling the weight of intention settle in like sand.

“A traveling almanac,” Kian said. “I thought—when the dishes break, or if they break again, or if the wires get chewed—” They caught their tongue between their teeth. “People walk between modules anyway. We could carry stories. And counts of beans. And where the rovers left ruts that haven’t been filled because rovers are rude. The knots are for numbers, and the colors are for types. The same way they did, in the old something.”

“Quipu,” Aya said, soft with delight. “Yes. We could do that. We could teach the teenagers to tie without looking. They could get paid in soup for every ten truths.”

“Can I leave this here?” Kian’s eyes were hopeful and blazing. “Or is it not anything yet.”

“It is everything,” Aya said. “It is the beginning of what a city does after a storm.”

They hung the belt at the entrance. The first entry was a count of surviving basil plants. The second was a recipe that required only two things the Moon could not provide. The third was a story in six knots that Tala laughed at so hard she had to sit down.

By the end of the month, the belt had shed its first length and needed splicing. People untied and re-tied strings with the tender irritation of someone undoing a necklace from a lover’s tossed clothes. Aya had to make a rule about who could put in a knot for death and who could untie one. It was not a rule she wanted to make, but cities have those and librarians must write them down.

On the day Kian’s grandmother’s station sent a packet that had bounced its way around three worlds and two moons and arrived limping, Aya called them in. They were taller; children are always taller too abruptly. They looked around the Hall as if asking the walls to be witnesses.

“We have a second voice,” Aya said. She handed them the earbone and did not, this time, try to listen first.

The voice was shorter still. It was a time-stamp and a laugh that cracked and then a sentence, which Aya caught over the breath Kian let out.

“Keeya,” the voice said. “I said the winds and I asked the question and they laughed and they showed me their hands.”

Kian took the earbone out and put it down as if it were a bug they were trying not to scare. They looked at Aya with a composure that had only a little to do with age.

“Can I tell you what the secret was?” they asked.

Aya, who had been many things and who had learned that there are secrets that belong only to the place where they were told, nodded. “You can tell me your version.”

“They said the sea teaches you that you cannot apologize to a wave. You can only learn how to stand.” Kian tapped the Stone with one knuckle, very lightly, because people who love a thing also like to test its sound. “We should write that down.”

“We will,” Aya said. “And also, we will say it. And also, we will make a game where you have to stand on one foot and let three people push you, and the one who laughs last gets to teach the next player.”

Kian grinned. “That is ridiculous.”

“That is how we remember.”

They wrote it. They told it. They taught the game. Murmur let them chuckle and sweat in the vestibule with what Aya would one day choose to believe was not disapproval but a careful interest in humidity.

In a year, people would call that storm the Spindle because Tala had compared it to thread and because someone had drawn it like a spindle on a wall and the name had stuck. In five years, the children born after would ask why their parents rolled their eyes when anyone licked a finger and held it up in the hall to feel the electric draft. In fifty years, the Stone would have another thousand layers. In a hundred, someone would stand where Aya stood and put their ear to a glass and think they could hear moondust settling, because people will make of silence an animal to pet. They would put names into tough tape with stiff fingers. They would hang belts and count basil and invent entirely new knottings that did not exist in any old hunger. They would teach a person who had never seen a sea that you can apologize without expecting a wave to stop.

On the day Aya’s hair turned the color of cellar salt, Kian came to the library early. They had something in their palm, curled like a bug asleep. They opened their hand and showed Aya a watch.

It did not tick. The face was cracked, the glass veined in a web that caught the light. Inside, the gears dormed. Someone had loved this thing by grinding it flat with thumb and time.

“I thought you could keep it,” Kian said. “Not in the catalog. On the shelf that isn’t a shelf.”

Aya took the watch. Her thumb made a new patina on old skin. She thought of Tala’s arm on her shoulder, of the roar of a cryobank refusing to die politely, of the way a small voice can cut through an entire storm. She thought, with a sense of rightness so simple it might have been invented by a child, that the library was bigger than its stacks and smaller than a person’s hand. She thought: a watch that doesn’t move is not broken if it still knows what a thumb feels like.

She put the watch on the shelf that wasn’t a shelf, the one inside her vest, over her heart. She stood under the old brass clock and counted, for no reason a machine could register as logical, to sixty, and when she had reached sixty she started again. She listened hard, not for ticks or for screams or for ghosts, but for the soft, steady, unambiguous sound of a person breathing next to a person, and when she found it, she kept it, which is to say, she gave it away. That is how archivists work. That is how storms pass. That is how, on a dry bone of a moon where nothing rusts without help, people learn to make weather.

And when, at last, a child who had not yet learned the names of winds came in carrying a knot too tight for their small fingers to loosen, Aya sat with them, pulled a stool close, took the belt in her lap, and said: tell me what this knot remembers, and I will teach you to untie it without breaking the string. Outside, the basil counted itself; above, the dishes listened; the Moon leaned a little toward the sun with all the impertinence of a rock that had outlived every kind of rumor. Inside, people breathed, which is to say: we were here. We remembered. We will be again.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
The first rule of the tideways is that you look down and ahead at the same time. Down to catch the angle of the slick on the panels, to feel the grain of barnacle on rubber under the soles of your boots; ahead to see who’s coming around the curve, which goat’s learned to undo its halter and is chewing the rope like sailor’s licorice, which vendor has found a new way to trick the gulls. The city remakes its edges every day. If you forget that for a moment, the water reminds you, up to the ankle if it’s forgiving, the hip if it’s not.

I leave before dawn, when the moss-wires that light the walkway are bleached and shivering. The chain on my cart sings a tired metal song. Cormorants lift and straighten wings on the ruined pylons of the old pier, ancient geometry solved in black, and somewhere far out in the dark a buoy taps the side of something hollow and patient. People say it’s the old ferry rusting under there, dreaming itself back into a boat.

I’ve got six Seeds to run today, pale ceramic capsules the size of hens’ eggs, sealed at their mouths with beeswax printed with whoever’s sigil bought the run. I carry them in a felt roll against my ribs, the better to keep the changes out of them, the better to know with my body when they get warm or cold or ask for attention. The second rule of the tideways is that you never open what you carry. The third is that you listen anyway.

“Sol,” old Mica at the assignment table says. She slides me the ledger board like she’s dealing a dangerous card. The market hums behind her in its particular pre-light key: fish breath and oil lamps, haggling muttered like prayer, eels looping lazily in tubs as children dare each other to touch their glassy heads. “You get a lonely one today. Map twenty-three.”

I want to ask her to say that again, but my mouth stays shut because my manners aren’t entirely rusted. She flicks a moth away from the ink pot with the back of her knuckle and watches my face with her one good eye. They say Mica’s other eye looks at an earlier version of the market, where the funny smells were illegal and the stalls were nailed down and no one used a bucket for a live bird. They say a lot of things.

“Edda at the Salt Library,” she adds, and that does it; people nearby turn their heads because they heard the name the way metal hears thunder. Of course they did. You can’t keep secrets about directions. It’s what they’re made to undo.

Mica flips the ledger to me so I can sign for the route. “You remember the channels by the old bus barn? The ones that make you think you’re walking when you’re actually going sideways?”

“I’ll go around,” I say, writing my initials small and neat. My hand has learned not to tremble when the current in the floor changes. It still wants to.

She grunts. “Good. The Mercators are sniffing at the west moorings. If you see them before they see you, it’ll be because they’re not looking.”

Which means they are. Which means they’ll either get there first and wait or send someone into my ribs with a hard knee and a friendly grin. The Mercators love maps the way gamblers love rigged cards. We’ve danced before. I go light up my shoulder with heat from the tea canister and slide the felt roll into my sash, and I set off with my cart’s handle in my hand and the ribs of the city’s morning settling around me like a half-buttoned shirt.

Reed is waiting by the seaweed combers, trying to look like no one owns him and he was just built into the market wall. He’s got the long careful hands of a maker and the narrow hungry face of someone who’s not yet learned how to feed himself on what he makes. He spots me and detaches from his failed impression of architecture and falls into step, one barefoot, one booted, because likely he lost a game for that boot and told himself it was a fair exchange for learning to be quicker.

“Take me,” Reed says. He always starts with that, because sometimes insistence shakes something loose. “Two quays and the stairs by the clam boats. I can do stairs faster than goats can chew through what you’re hauling.”

“Goats don’t chew stairs,” I tell him, which is exactly the kind of disappointment you’re supposed to hand a kid every hour on the hour until he folds and waits his turn to be old. “And I don’t hump Maps with children. Go pester the letter-lifters. They love questions.”

He makes a face that means not a chance, and under it I can see the other answer, the one he won’t say around me no matter how long I hold out sea-urchin tongues at the urge to pry: that the letter-lifters look for ways to slip out of their jobs while their work is looking the other way. Reed wants to be a courier because we do what we do the whole time we’re doing it. We point ourselves at the edge of the city. We go until the city stops moving in that direction, and then we decide what to do next very quickly, and then we either jump or we don’t.

“You’ll carry a rope for the eel ladder,” I say, before I can stop my mouth from betraying the rest of me. “That’s all. Gulls farmed my last one like it was corn. You tell me when a Mercator has his shoulders hunched, and you don’t talk to anyone who gives you a discount.”

He tries not to smile. It goes very badly. He takes the rope from my cart and coils it like it’s asleep and we move.

The northern causeways are taut with new growth. Everything that can grow tries here, especially if you don’t want it to, especially if you cleared it yesterday and told it to stay put. The city’s menagerie is out, ratters and cats and the feral little lines of yard dogs that have rearranged themselves into tribes over the years, all of them negotiating for the place where the fishmongers’ rubbish will fall and the shrimp shells will be dumped and the gentlemen who will never work a day in their lives will spit expensive bones into the slats. People are making wagers over gull-landing grids on tarpaulin, counting the birds like beans. If you squint, that particular piece of hustle still looks like a temple.

We take the split around the old bus barn even though it adds minutes, because the channels under those panels were laid by someone who was asked to sabotage traffic and obliged with a craftsman’s pride. The air goes cool and then thin as we step around the bulk of concrete. Reed touches it with his knuckles the way boys touch things that might be giants or spells, his face tipped up as if something might be hiding on the ceiling. The barn’s paint is a skin you could peel. Under it are the names of the bus routes, the numbers long since worn away by salt. Eighth Avenue to the river, the words say. City Medical to the end of days.

At Twine Quay, we stop for rope because I told myself I would not do another month of patching tears with prayers. The rope-seller is a woman of smaller than average height and a stare like a window in winter. She sells lengths that gleam with old fish scales and lengths that feel like holding a friend’s wrist. I buy two. Reed makes himself useful and counts in breaths while she measures, which is an old way and a fast one, and it pleases her. For that, she gives him a tether for free, a short length with a brass hook like a comma.

“For him and not for you,” she tells me, as if I’d spend my day trying to wring fifty centimeters of line out of a child. She’s not wrong to worry. “If you teach him to be stingy, the city will tan him with her smallest hands. If you teach him to be generous, he might make it to next spring with both eyes.”

I tuck the rope into my cart. The light has crept up and spilled itself messily on the wet steel. The tide is turning, the worst of the change already spent. You can hear it in the moan under the footings. If we hustle, we can crest the lifting section by the third inlet before the mechanism grows stubborn. If we don’t hustle, we’ll be hauling ourselves up with our new rope, one hand over the other, and my knees will have a few pungent words to say about that.

Two groans later, Reed is pointing with the kind of delicacy that suggests he believes in my peripheral vision and also in my capacity for denial. The Mercator boys in their proper vests stand around an upturned crate scratching a compass on the wood with a nail. They look like fishermen who have recently had their hair talked about by expensive combs. I walk the cart with all the solemnity of a small, uninteresting miracle, looking down and ahead. If they want me, they’ll step in front and wax philosophical about equitable contracts until I’ve forgotten what I came here to do. If they don’t, one of them will finger his sleeve and mark us for later. Either way, Reed is learning what he came to learn.

A Seed warms against my ribs.

It’s the faintest change, the kind you could ascribe to embarrassment or a broken patch of sun, except that it’s the Map twenty-three capsule and the warmth is close to a tremor. My skin reads it as if it were a hand. I keep my eyes on the hinge bolts ahead and my breath routine. Nobody needs to know what a courier feels. The second rule makes that true even when the third one wants to hum.

“Keep,” I tell Reed under my breath, which is short for keep your face, keep your feet, keep your hope inside your mouth, the way you’d tell someone at the edge of a drop to keep their hat, like that could stop the wind. He tucks his loose hair behind his ear and nods, and we push into the hinge’s shadow, and I remember with complete clarity the man's laugh when I fell from a dock at fourteen and went under and the world made a fast second language out of cold.

The Salt Library sits on what used to be a desalination plant, which felt like a kind of circular joke even before Edda stuffed its every dry crevice with the names of lost ways. Its old intake pipes sprout from its spine like ribs, stained orange at the mouths as if it learned to smoke late in life. From the panels above, you can see the whole shuffle of the eastern shallows and the black glass of the yacht-wreck that left the city an expensive tooth in a cheap grin. You can see, if you know where to look, the path of the water past its pipes in a onetime year when the tide went in and then forgot how to leave, and the city held its breath until someone remembered that you can only be a guest at a party where everything drowns in sugar for so long.

Edda herself is on the roof with a colander on her head. It is not a hat. It is an instrument. She holds it by its two handles like a crown she does not owe the world. When she sees me, she names the wind. She raises an eyebrow halfway up her lined forehead at Reed, who has managed to make his scruffiest shirt somehow look like it’s trying to remember how to be a flag.

“Come up or don’t, but don’t do that thing where you stand on the top step and think your virtue could breathe this air for you,” Edda calls, at exactly the moment my legs are considering whether my virtue might. “I’m tracking a resonance and pretending the sound doesn’t know I’m a spy. It’s the only way to woo a tone with self-respect.”

“I brought you a Map,” I say, because a single clear sentence can pin a whole room into a particular present. “Twenty-three.”

She takes the colander off her head and pretends she doesn’t pluck a few stray leaves from its holes. “Don’t say the number in that tone. That tone invites people to step on your insteps.”

“They’re already eager,” I say. From the roof, I can see the Mercators down the way, looking like men who are practicing not looking like men at all, who are practicing being fog.

“Good,” she says, as if I’d told her a baker brought her a fresh heart. “They’ll take care of standing there and not being helpful. Come in. I have something to show you that you have brought me to show you.”

Inside the Library, the air is a body and it holds you hard. Edda keeps salt from a hundred coasts, brick-sized and fig-soft and translucent, cataloged with the precision you’d use for docile weapons. She taps them with the nails of her first two fingers and hears what they remember. She is teaching a few people to do it. The room smells like a stable and the bottom of a tidepool. It smells like if you licked a blade and then ate a plum.

“You’ve felt it,” she says, before I can wedge my sentence into a space that looks like it could hold it. “The Map asking to be touched and refusing to be owned.”

“I haven’t opened it,” I say, flaring with the reflex of my rule like a fish flashing its belly at a shark.

“I didn’t say you had. I said you felt it. If you hadn’t, you wouldn’t have brought it to me before you brought it to me.”

Edda’s like that. She shears the fluff from things but leaves enough meat on to be mannerly about it. I don’t know how old she is. She was my age when I was the Reed of someone else, and if you ask her about the before-times when maps were flat like a lie and a map of a corporation could own a river if you said the words in a courtroom with the right shape of mouth, she will tell you that the soul of a place is not included in any title. She can be exhausting to listen to if you don’t like being made larger.

I get out the felt roll and let her take the capsule marked with beeswax like a yellow thumbprint. When she lifts it, the warmth of it leeches the light. I swear to you, the room goes dim as if the wax itself had called the sun close enough to say there were things it wanted to say without witnesses. Edda doesn’t flinch. She holds the Seed like a small clean animal. Reed forgets his breath is a thing he has to fetch every few seconds and I reach out and take his wrist and squeeze until the body remembers it should.

She hums. It’s not a tune. It’s a location in a throat. The capsule answers her with a delicate beat. If it were a hand it would be tapping a table to say yes. If it were a bird it would be lifting its neck feathers and flattening them in turn. If it were words you could hear without damaging them, it would be telling you to tear off the lid and read it with your fingers and your teeth.

“I can’t,” I say, before my mouth can begin the sin of the next thing. “It’s not for my mouth.”

“I know,” Edda says, and I let go of the breath my fingers were strangling in Reed’s arm. “It’s not for mine either. Not alone. You brought it to the correct mistake.”

We stand very still. The Mercators outside shift their weight from one foot to the other, and you can hear it as if the city had been fitted with the bones of egrets. The Map hums in Edda’s small hard hands. She rests it against the square of salt nearest the east wall, the one that remembers a warm fog that never lifted. The beeswax print darkens.

“What if,” Reed says, and then stops dead like he just realized he was using up his future’s sharpest knives on a room that would give them back polished. “What if it’s not a drawing?”

“Then we will fail to look for lines,” Edda says. “Then we will look for the space between.”

Time sits down and puts its chin in its hands. I have delivered meals that went cold and promises that exploded. I have delivered a bird to a boy who did not know how to hold grief without making it bite. I have delivered my own name to someone who used it later in a way that made it heavier and also easier to turn. I have never stood with a thing that made my palms sweat from the idea of it.

Outside, thunder unpacks itself with the bored patience of an aunt who brought the wrong dress to the wrong dance and is determined to find fun. The tide at the Library’s base sucks and fills. You can hear the gate in the channels by the bus barn ratchet open to gawk. We are on a clock whose hand is a mouth.

“Tell me the rule,” Edda says. She turns to me and makes her small face very ordinary. “Put it in my hand like you would put the spine of a fish in a cat’s.”

I say, “Don’t open what you carry.”

“Why not?”

“Because it’s not yours.”

“What is it?”

“A route,” I say, and realize as I dress my own throat in the word that I have not been carrying letters or ceramic or code. I have been carting future from one body to another.

“Right,” she says. “Open it anyway, Sol.”

I look at her like she’s broken her teeth on a law. “You just—”

“I said the rule that belonged to yesterday,” she says. “This one’s to the city. It came to you because your hands know when to be wrong.”

Reed opens his mouth. I shut it with a look. Then, because I can smell the rain like iron and flowers and I can hear a boat running its engine without going anywhere and I can feel in my fourth rib the way the causeway will topple if we wait to choose until the water has already chosen for us, I take out my knife and I pry up the edge of the beeswax with the care you would use to borrow a word you aren’t sure you know the whole of. The wax gives. The smell of living paper hits us in the teeth.

Inside is not ink. Inside is a pattern woven in cords so fine a frog would blush to call them thread. They have been dyed with lichens and heat and words. They glisten the way rotted wood glistens when you whisper its real name. If I breathed too hard, I could bruise them. Edda’s hands hover as if she’s about to catch a plate someone flung at a wedding and she doesn’t want to make a mess. Reed swears very politely.

The pattern clicks in my head with a sensation like a very old box with a false bottom finally letting go because you have got its riddle all the way. It is not a map of streets. It is not a set of coordinates to a cache of dignity some old mayor thought he hid at the foot of a rare tree. It is a score. It is a song that a machine will understand. It is instructions for telling the river a joke it will want to keep repeating.

“There,” Edda breathes, the way you do when you’ve been waiting for two winters for one herb to throw a new leaf. “Yes.”

“What is it?” Reed asks, reverent, terrified to say a wrong word and make the soft parts of the instruction curl and die like question ferns in a dry season.

Edda taps with the nail of her first finger, counting without moving her lips. A sequence. A cadence. I see the notes like stones. I see where they ask for bodies, for hands, for the breath of people. I see the way the tide gates under our feet could be told to open in a harmony that is older than civic duty and more precise than law. I see rope, not to bind but to make a throat. I see stones, but not the big ones we use for throwing at the idea of a problem. I see the little basalt boulders, the ones wedged under causeway supports, the ones that ring when goats clamber them with their horny feet.

“You put this into the gates?” Reed says, eyes wide enough to show the white all around. “And then the flood doesn’t take the west homes?”

“Then the flood and the city talk,” Edda says softly. “Then we stop lecturing water like it was born yesterday.”

My stomach flips and lands. The Mercators would make a document from this and stamp it with their rings and sell its application to streets block by block like rain. They would drape the city in leases and then, when the river tired of their tone, they would sue it in their dreams. There are people who know how to make a virtue out of only helping when it is measured. I am not patient enough to watch them practice.

We carry the Map to the east gate—the small one first. The big ones are for when you have time to learn and, additionally, luck. The small one is a toothbrush you keep by the sink because you are fond of your teeth. The Salt Library vibrates all through the going, like a cat you haven’t fed soon enough who wants to make certain you remember where it keeps its gratitude. Outside, the boil of incoming weather flounces its skirts. People look up and decide in one look whether their baskets are waterproof, whether their grandmothers lied when they said to plan, whether they should go later to the place they go when they hope no one sees them cry.

“We’re not building anything,” Edda tells the small knot of watchers we always collect when we move a loaded object with reverent idiocy. “We’re playing a thing. If you have hands that are not busy, give me two years of your life in five minutes.”

She sets the cords on a crate and gestures like a conductor until the knot grows teeth. She takes rope from my cart—my fine new rope, the one that still smells like someone's quiet cussing—and she’s making a series of loops across the mouth of the channel. You cannot stand too close to where she’s pointing or the water will resent it. The basalt lumps sit at either side, black as swallowed secrets. She places the cord as the pattern suggests. When she does it, the wind says yes through the leaves of a plant that grew where it wasn’t asked to and turned itself into a doctrine by force.

“Reed!” she says, and the boy steps like a dog into a new job and is surprised that the collar feels like a crown. “The stones. The fat ones that remember volcano. The flat ones that remember songs. Bring three. No, six. No, bring what your arm doesn’t think it can.”

He runs like someone taught him to flee the bad parts of himself. He returns with stones cradled against his chest, a grunt squeezed through his teeth. He sets them down and doesn’t look at anyone until his breath comes back to its normal shape. Edda hums. The Gate hums back. The men from the Mercators upshift their shoulders, their bodies sliding into the posture of men who are about to say they are here to help you not make a mistake with the object you bravely purchased with your own blood.

“Whatever you are doing,” the tallest one says, because making conversation with a hurricane is in his contract, “we could help you make it legal.”

“Legality is the past tense of appetite,” Edda says, and then, to us, gentle like calling a cat’s name when it’s embedded itself under a bed, “Now.”

We pull and let go, pull and let go, in the time the Map has taught our muscles. The rope becomes a throat. The stones sing. The water at the gate hesitates, flips a coin, considers making a point, and then it slides not easily but with that same subservient happiness you see in an animal that wanted to bolt and then remembered the warmth of the stable’s straw. It flows in harmony, if not yet in friendship. It does not pout and shove. It moves in phrases. The first lash of the storm hits, hard and full-mouthed. The rope thrums. The stones in the walls answer with a sound that is not made by polite materials. The people around us look at the water like it just used their first name without asking.

The flood does not take the west homes.

It does not wade past waist-heights and collect shoes. It does not drown the potted herbs and carry the smallest dogs around and make men shout into rain as if rain could be scolded. It hits the gates, and the gates, told to sing instead of yank, talk back to it, and the water rolls under them like a chair pulled out for an aunt who has had a day and is still too proud to sit while you stand. The current sits. The current rises. The current goes where there is room to go. I cannot remember being this obediently in love with physics.

Around us, the people who came because nothing else was able to hold them stand very straight. One of the Mercators steps in toward Edda with his hands out like he’s going to put a coat around her shoulders. I move in a way that tells him I remember my wooden self at fourteen and a hand in my breastbone and the water and the laugh, and that if he touches the woman he will get to see the inside of one of the stones up close. It doesn’t come to that. The storm keeps us honest. He steps back with a small wounded sigh any other day would have made me stop and ask who failed to hold him when he was a boy.

“What is it called?” Reed breathes, who needs names like bread.

“Nothing yet,” Edda says. “Everything is just itself until enough mouths agree to call it something and then live like that name makes sense. Help me tune it. The C by the old scales has a wobble.”

We do. We tune the gate like a throat. We tune the mechanisms inside the walls with hammers that have been used to end small arguments for forty years. We sing. I add a low hum in my bones because if the world is going to pretend I am a man of useful mass, I should prove it something at least once in my day. The gate answers. The stones grow warm because a property you may not know about basalt is that it remembers old fire the way some of us remember old insults: not by recounting them, but by becoming a version of ourselves sharper than any apology.

When the rain slants to the side and gets thin, when the day shakes itself like a dog and everything comes back as itself, wet and a little astonished, we let the rope rest. We walk the edges of the gate with our fingers and find nothing about to betray us except time. Even a song corrodes. We will have to keep teaching it to say please and thank you. That suits me. Some jobs end. Some loop like a good knot. I am built for both and I’d forgotten the shape of that pride, how it stands with its hands under what it loves, ready to lift or shove as needed.

The Mercators do not clap. They stand not looking, which is worse. You can hear the air around them curdle with what they won’t name. They leave like men who think that if they move in a certain way they will appear not to be moving at all. Edda watches them with the same mild curiosity you would afford a gull who has noticed a new fruit. Then she wipes the cords with the side of her sleeve and rolls them like bread dough into a waiting basket and sets its handle across my forearms.

“Take it to the Cruz Gate before the moon forgets what side of the planet it’s on,” she says. “And to the flats by the old pigment shed. And to the houses in the knees of the big road at Gully. And—”

“Stop,” I tell her. “Breathe. The city is not an esophagus.”

“We’ll see,” she says. She pats my cheek like my mother didn’t and may have wanted to on a very specific day when I was an impossible boy with a new scar. “This was never going to be just for me. Tell Mica to write down who came and pulled and let go. If someone asks me later if I hoarded a way to sing to the water, my ghost will laugh.”

We spend the rest of the day being a different kind of courier. Reed nearly drops a hammer on his own toes and saves himself by unlearning grace. We run between gates, a chain of swapping hands and sweat. We teach. The song turns stubborn in different places at each mouth and we learn that no one tune fits every hinge. The basalt stones we use are cousins. We listen to them to learn their arguments. When the sun goes down, the city glows under us like a set of patient embers. You can see the weave of gutters where it’s been too many years since the last man who claimed to be in charge agreed to allocate time for what looks like a small job.

At the day’s end, back at the Library, Edda sits with her hands open on her knees and her lips slightly parted. She is an old woman and also not anything like that. She is new in the same way the cord pattern is old. The room seems larger, but it’s possible it’s just that my breath is a better size. Reed leans against a salt block and closes his eyes for exactly a count of three and then opens them as if he’d been given permission to carry them a little further.

“Sol,” Edda says. “Put down the wrong you did and pick up the right that made it possible. If anyone comes to you later and says you broke your rule, tell them you delivered it as wide as it needed to go.”

I nod. I do inventory in my head of the small sins I collected today and the larger one I used to crush their throats. There is a weight you choose to carry and there is weight that sits in you because you once chose something else and the choice thought it could take up permanent lodging. I have hauled both. Today the first kind wins.

At the Tide Market the next morning, people talk about the sound in the bones of the city when the water came and did not take their tables away. They talk about music like it could be legislation. Mica raises her one good eyebrow when I bring her the ledger instead of the shorter note I leave her on days when my muscles are too tired to be formal. She squints at my entries and shakes her head as if I’d been writing with my feet.

“You didn’t write down who, you wrote down how,” she says.

“I know,” I say, because the names felt paltry dust to what happened. “This is a ledger of instructions now. We can’t depend on Edda’s hands if she is asleep when the thing asks. She’s frightening, but she’s not omnipresent.”

“She will be when she’s dead,” Mica says. “The good ones always are, and so are the bad ones, but death insists on equal treatment. Sol, I am too old to be fluttered by your dramas, but be careful where you set down your sore righteous shoulder. It will not always be applauded when you teach a thing to breed.”

“I can listen to the people who hate my methods,” I say, “and also I can take the rope to Gully.”

She taps the paper. “Good. And teach Reed to say no to questions that are actually nets. He’s pretty now, and all the worst nets like pretty.”

I groan because I am not immune to useful warnings spoken in the tone of someone who watched you climb a pilaster at eight and break your tooth and then was expected to feel bad for you. I go outside and find Reed and give him the speech Mica told me to give him and he rolls his eyes exactly like I rolled mine into the appropriate quadrant of the future, and we go. The water moves under our feet at the gate not like a threat but like an argument in progress, the pleasant kind, the kind that ends in mutual affection and quick laughter, the kind that carries away your plates while you’re still talking because you forgot to notice you finished eating.

In the days that follow, we share the Map the way you share a new swear that is delicious in the mouth. We include and add. Someone realizes the shells of the old fan-cooled exchangers down by the welded ship will carry the lower overtones better than the basalt does, lighter in the hand and less stubborn and not a hazard to toes. An old woman no one has seen without a hat in forty years sings a counter-hum in B-flat at Bonnet Gate and makes the water there nervous enough to behave itself in company. A boy in a chair that likes to roll discovers that the pattern calls him to be the anchor at the highest not the lowest stretch, that what he can hold is not related to where his body runs out. None of us thought to keep it quiet. Some of us think to tell the Mercators what they could do to be at peace with developments. It turns out most of them are better at not being around when they can’t write the bill.

I go home some nights so tired I dream of being a fish and loving the net. I wake up with my mouth on a rope burn and laugh and it doesn’t make my ribs hurt. On a day when thunder drills this city like a new tooth, we get a visitor from the upriver farms with a basket of radishes and the look of a person who learned old math from an aunt and wants to repay the loan by making two old ways marry. She sits at a gate with us and teaches us the timing that the river uses when it has drunk too much snow. We teach her the note that makes the gate want to help. She goes back and they put a new span over the trickle-drain and it holds because a wall is a bad instrument and a bridge is a good one.

Reed learns to say no with his mouth closed, his palms flat. He learns to say yes with an economy that makes people lean in. He also learns that having a tether that is his alone is like having a secret that is good. He stops trying to follow me on runs to places that would look at his face and want to do it harm just because it can. He starts walking on his own to the old bus barn and standing at the mouth of the bent channel and making friends with its particular desire to see him stumble, and then he goes a day without slipping. He tells me very politely and I tell him very impolitely that I am proud. We adjust.

Mica puts a little copper nail by my name on the ledger by the thing where she keeps her tea. I don’t ask her what it means. I know. Some people put their nails in their ears. Some of us put them in doors. It’s a mark that says: if you have something that needs to be carried that could change the posture of the city around itself, this person will be wrong in the correct direction when the rule makes itself too small. It also says: when this person fails—and I will—don’t let them think they can wriggle away from their own damage. A copper nail is chemistry and a plan.

The Mercators are not defeated. Every week a new version of an old document springs up somewhere promising to codify the fair distribution of helpfulness. The documents have very good grammar. It is so good that it makes you forget to ask where your hands went when the page ended. A cousin of mine—I have many, the way a tree has secrets it calls cousins if you sit under it long enough and sing—tells me that a new office open somewhere up in the north quarter is drawing neat maps and rewarding men in neat vests for the neatness as if neatness were a virtue and not sometimes a sickness. We do not burn offices. We learn to teach the rope to sing over them. Sometimes it’s even enough.

I have never been a lover of one thing only. When I was a boy I had a jar of buttons and I loved each one in a way that would embarrass the part of me that later learned to let go. I loved being near the water and I loved being nowhere near it. I loved old stories and I loved the idea that I could make one and then take it apart later to see where it bent. People tell me that to be a courier is to be forever between. That’s not wrong. It’s also to choose. I choose a little too often for those who prefer to wait. But then a night like the first gate night comes, and the water chewing its old cigar of hate makes a different decision because our rope taught it a better joke, and I sleep like someone who can regret in the morning, which is an opulent way to live. I recommend it.

If you come to the city now, they will tell you we were always like this. People are kind to themselves in their history. That’s all right. They will point out the stones at the gates and tell you their mother brought one with her from a field upriver that remembered horses. They will say their aunt sang the low hum at Lamp Gate until her front teeth tingled and then she cried because she’s shy and it surprised her to come apart. They will tell you they were on the panels when the big gate at Cruz moaned and then laughed and the river took a breath and then gave it back and their name got sharper afterward. They will not tell you that they thought for a minute they should wait for permission. They will not tell you that we almost preferred the complicated suffering we were used to, because it had our shape.

Sometimes I see the Mercators down at the Quay buying spools of line and pretending the purchase is homemade. Sometimes I see their vests hung up behind laundry on lines outside houses whose women who would steal their breath if they lay down long enough to make that use of their hands. Sometimes they stand for the hum. I do not trust them with the rope, but I do not stop them from humming. It’s not ours. It never was.

The other day I carried a Seed that wheezed like a pair of old bellows being flattered back into work. Inside was an address and a name and an apology. I walked it up to a door that had lost the paint on its lower half to a mouth of brine. The woman there took it with hands that had been washed in flour and she kissed the Wax like it was a living back. She did not open it while I watched. I turned away and Reed watched me turn away, and I could see him measuring my restraint against the day I cut my rule with a knife. Good. He is a better ledger for me than the book.

In a few seasons the rope at Bonnet will fray and we will need a new one and it will be a small scandal how slowly they will repair it because the man who sells the best rope will have taken to his bed. In a few seasons we will teach someone else to tap the salt blocks and hear how a wind moved through a channel before a channel knew to call itself anything but dirt. In a few seasons we will be wrong again, correctly, we can hope. There will be a run of days when the missing boot Reed lost will come back to his foot in the shape of a story about a stranger who gave him a different shoe and told him he would always be walking into a new kind of weather. He will tell me this appalling anecdote and I will grind my molars and then I will laugh because he’s right.

On the morning that the moon makes a fool of a harbor-master by being late to a meeting it did not agree to attend, I wake early, earlier than my bones have yet learned to tolerate with grace, and I go alone to the first gate we taught. I sit on the sun-warmed edge and I put my palm flat on the stone at the place where the rope’s shadow falls when the sun is the small size I like best. The water licks the channel like a cat saying there you are, like a friend being polite. I hum the low note because I am sentimental at stupid hours. The gate answers in its polite yes. I do not cry. I also do not not cry. It is like that, sometimes, when the place you live accepts a small part of you as an organ it didn’t know it needed.

I am a courier. I carry the city when it is too light in one place and too heavy in another. Sometimes what I carry needs to be kept shut. Sometimes it needs a thumb under the lid and a knife at the beeswax and a hand steady enough to hold a soft pattern open to light while the wind puts its entire stupid opinion into the matter like a meddlesome aunt. The rule was not made to be broken. It was made to remind me that rules are maps and maps are not the land and do not own it and if you put your faith in them without humor you will drown where the street sign told you to walk.

The tideway panels shine. The goats have chewed exactly three centimeters off a rope they did not understand but regarded as a challenge. Mica will scold them like grandchildren. Edda will be on her roof with her foolish crown, looking like the queen of a thing we were supposed to inherit but were too busy to fetch. Reed will be somewhere with both feet, both his own, under him, and the curve of his mouth loose with a secret of his own that I had nothing to do with. The Mercators will be drawing tidy hairlines on real dirt, and the rain will lean across the city to remind us not to be tragic when completely ordinary adjustment will do.

You can bring me your Map. You can ask me to keep it secret and to teach it to sing in the same week. You can ask me to help you not drown. You can ask me to deliver yourself to yourself, which is all any of us does for anyone who’s not bringing us rope and food. I will say yes as often as my lungs still stretch. And when someone stands at the hinge of a city they love and hums something at it that makes it want to grow a new mouth, I will put my hand on their shoulder the way you put a shell at the mouth of a child and say listen, listen, listen.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I like getting lost. Not the panicky kind of lost that tightens your chest while the last bar of cell service wobbles, but the gentler disorientation that comes from taking a street because it has a friendly curve. My earliest memory of being lost is in a department store that smelled like raincoats and perfume. My mother turned a corner and vanished, and what had been aisles became corridors, what had been displays became walls.
