He had a barometer’s face, round and polished, and a way of moving that made you think all his bones were on springs. His hands were nicked and perfect, the hands of a person accustomed to screwing lids gently but firmly, to persuading hurricanes into glass. Everything was shelves, floor to ceiling, rows of jars and bottles, some with proper labels, some with grease pencil scribbles, some with the inscrutable handwriting of weather itself—loopy and wide, insisting on margins you couldn’t see.

“To browse,” I said, because it felt wrong to declare intention here. He nodded as if browsing were a sacred sacrament.

A jar hummed under my palm. I picked it up. Inside, the air trembled like a plucked string. The label, in the light that tilted from the high windows, read: Distant Heat Lightning, Plains, 1974. Keep away from curtains. Another shelf offered more intimate atmospheres: Behind-the-Ear Warmth, Winter, Father’s Coat; Hallway Cool, Between Parties; Bus Stop Breath, February. One was just called Ordinary Tuesday, with a note: Strong effect. Use in small room.

“What does Ordinary Tuesday do?” I asked. My voice was softer than usual. The shop required listening with all parts of the face.

He took it from me, held it up as if revealing something to the jar itself. “It makes a day taste the way days used to when you were not measuring them. Coffee that is merely coffee. Mail that is mostly catalogs. It lets you remember how to be unspecial, which is an exquisite relief.” He placed it back carefully. “Not for everyone.”

“Do people ask for Friday nights? For birthday storms? For boldness?”

“Boldness is a pressure system anyone can summon, if they’re willing to stand naked in a wind that has no interest in their modesty.” He smiled with only one side of his mouth. “But yes. People ask for certainties with a high chance of applause. They buy most of the sunsets.” He gestured upwards with his chin. “We’re low, as you see.”

Above me, a shelf of stained bottles glowed with the last light of days gone sweet-orange and feathery. I thought of the hollowness in me, of its steady, practical complaint, and imagined uncorking a sunset into it. I imagined it settling there like a gold coin.

“I can’t sell you another person’s weather,” he said, as if he had plucked the thought from my shoulder. “The jars with names—those are private licenses. Heaven forbid someone strolls out with your grandmother’s porch wind.”

“Then what do you sell?”

“What the world forgets to notice,” he said. “What is leftover when awe gets tired. Also, what excess the big weather sheds. I skim the foam from storms that overboil. I catch the misfit breezes that don’t belong to any climate we are currently making.”

I walked the aisles. There was Drawer Breeze (Sock), which smelled faintly of cedar and dust and hopes to match. There was Air Under Newsprint, which when I held it close to my nose made me think of subway rides where everyone is collectively, silently convinced of something. I saw Aisle Twenty-One at the Store (Fluorescents and Apples), and it was so precisely itself that my throat did a little ache.

“What happens if one breaks?” I asked.

“Depends on the floor,” he said. “On pine, you get forgiveness. On tile, a spectacle. On concrete, a short sermon and then nothing.”

There were advisory notices in his little script, tucked into corners: Do not store fog next to sugar. Keep anise away from north winds to avoid licorice storms. If you borrow June, return July. I wanted to ask how one returned a month, and decided I would understand later or never.

“How much for a pocket of summer?” I said, half teasing.

He considered me as if my question had scale and contour. “For you, summer is too loud just now. You are overfull of narratives that advertise themselves. What you need won’t say its name. It will arrive wearing someone else’s coat. It will sit at your table and eat your oranges and you won’t recognize hunger until it sighs in the chair.”

I touched the smooth shoulder of a bottle labeled Quiet at 3:07 a.m. The liquid inside was colorless. When I tipped it, a flock of moths stitched a small constellation against the glass. My bloodstream slowed in a way that felt diagnostic.

“I lost something,” I said, because the shop permitted true statements if spoken like facts about the weather. “And the days are either made of noise or holes. I cannot match them.”

He nodded in the way of a man who has stitched up centuries. “Try this.” He reached for a squat jar with a blue rubber seal. Its glass was clouded from within, like breath on a window in a house where good soups happen. The label was no label, only a small chalk dot.

“What is it?”

“The minute before you realize you are not lost,” he said. “Unhappened panics. Streets that straighten. Keys found behind the bowl of other keys. No promises of how; only a softness around the future tense.”

“How much?” I asked, already ridiculous, already certain I was ridiculous.

“One thing,” he said. “Your story about rain on a day that should not have rained. Tell it to this jar. It likes true things.”

I told it. I told him how the parade still happened, the floats like stubborn islands, my cousin in paper wings stomping wet confetti back into celebration, the brass section swallowing water and blowing it out as music anyway. The minute felt like a room where I could lower my voice and be understood.

He listened the way fields listen to clouds: patient, without promise. He took the jar, pressed its lid to his ear. “Good,” he said. “It ate well.” He slid it across the counter.

I carried it home on the bus, cradled in my coat as if transporting a creature that could sleep or wake me. I watched the city’s breath fog the windows and saw my own small humidity in the corner. Everyone else was a weathervane: heads turning in unison as the bus sighed to each stop, chins tilted to read a sky we had built and called useful.

At my table I set the jar down. I listened to my refrigerator announce its coldness. I opened a drawer and closed it again. I stood, finally, and cracked the seal.

At first, nothing but a hush, like someone far above me had shuffled into a slipper. Then the room became larger by an eyelash. The air touched the back of my neck and apologized to no one. There was a clatter somewhere downstairs, not alarming, a human sound weathered by practice. I could feel the shape of my apartment change from a container for echo to a container for breath.

I had not realized how long I had been holding my shoulders like parentheses. They uncurled. The hollow thing inside, practical as a hinge, took a breath of something that smelled like pencil shavings and warmed stone. And then, briefly, the smell of oranges.

The jar did not spill light or launch a symphony. It permitted gravity to be ordinary and full of grace. It let the minutes be vowels instead of answers. It was nothing you could brag about at dinner, which is to say it was a gift I could keep.

In the morning, the sky was unsure of itself and the street glittered with last night’s bottles. When I passed Borrowed Climates, its shutters were down and papered over with a sign that was not there. It said nothing that answered questions. The air was the air, bold and indifferent. I was a person walking through it. The day had that Tuesday taste, or I did. I went to buy oranges. I paid for them with coins that were mine. I carried them home, and they were heavy and bright and exactly what they were.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I wake before the alarms, as if some small part of me keeps sentinel for the day. The room is dim, the window a rectangle of weather and rumor. In the pipes, water clears its throat. I reach for the notebook that waits by the bed and, without looking, draw a line straight across the first page, a horizon to measure myself against.

Someone laughs three floors down, a short bright sound that dissolves at once. A cat patrols the alley with a soldier’s gravity. I sip water and pretend it is the beginning of a myth. The spine straightens. The air keeps its promise to be ordinary and full of miracles I will only name by accident.

On the desk there is a small stone I picked up on a beach where the wind taught every object to bow. The stone is gray with a vein of white, like a syllable inside a whisper. People gave it names all day, a turtle, a cloud, a loaf of bread. I kept none of them. It has one job here, to remind me that noticing is a kind of prayer.

There are days when language is a river, and there are days when it is a locked cupboard with a key you swallowed in your sleep. Today is a third thing, a slow kite wind that pretends to be nothing at all until the paper lifts and tugs. I let it tug. The sentence will discover what it wants by the time I finish pretending I chose it.

Once, when I was nine, I buried a note in the backyard to see if time would answer it. I cannot remember what I wrote, only that I folded the paper until it became a small confident square and put it in a jar with two coins and a rubber band. I dug beside the maple, which did not object. Decades later, my mother sold the house, and the answer went on living without me.

I think about this more than I admit. How often we set something in the ground, then walk away convinced that the soil is an archive that loves us. Maybe it is. Maybe the jar opened of its own accord, and the coins let the note buy a word or two in the slow market of worms. Maybe a root read the message and steered the tree toward what it needed to hear.

I go to the window. The alley is a museum where this morning is the only exhibit. A woman in a yellow coat walks by carefully, as if she is balancing a cup of still water on her head. She looks up once, and I raise my hand without thinking. She raises hers, surprised, then laughs at herself and keeps going. We are both witnesses auditioning for a small role in each other’s story.

The kettle clicks off. I had not realized I had set it to work. This happens daily, the body performing a modest miracle while the mind runs errands in a city that does not exist. I pour tea and think of the first oranges brought across an ocean, how sailors tucked them in their jackets like suns they could keep. The peel, the oil, the hand polished by trying to know sweetness without eating it.

In the news, there will be things that scorch. But the radio is quiet on the shelf, and I am briefly not responsible for the whole earth. This is important to say. There is a guilt that pretends to be love and stands between us and our work, our families, our lunches, our afternoons of mending. I am learning to ask it to step aside. Not to leave, only to let the door open.

The tea is too hot. I put it down and open the notebook again. The horizon I drew has begun to look like a road that refuses to be straight. Fair enough, I think, and write the first true thing I can bear to write: I am here. Then the second: I do not know what comes next. After that, everything eases. The page forgives me for pretending I was ever in charge.

A friend once told me that birds do not sing because they are happy, they sing because the song is how a bird becomes itself in public. I do not know if this is biologically true, but I keep it in my pocket like a river stone. On days like this, when the world is a slow hallway with many dark doors, the song reaches under each knob and leaves a warm coin of light.

Later I will go outside and be a small mammal among larger inventions. I will buy pears with freckles and a loaf that remembers the hands that made it. I will send a message to a person I miss and refuse to apologize for missing them. I will carry a bag that lists, that learns my gait and tries to match it. All of this will pass for bravery, and in a way it is.

For now, though, I choose this chair as my continent. The grain of the wood is a coastline I could walk for days. My neighbors are beginning their negotiations with the day: a broom, a radio, a bicycle that coughs once, twice, then dares the hill. Somewhere a train rewrites the air. Somewhere a child declares a law and two plastic horses agree. Somewhere a new grief opens like a door and the room has no ceiling.

The stone waits. The tea cools. The horizon road bends and, in bending, admits that turning is not failure. I have a hundred chores and a thousand questions and at least one promise I do not know how to keep. Still, I think I will keep it. The day will not need my permission to proceed, but it listens when I say thank you. I say it now, and again, and again.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
This morning I woke to the smell of rain that had not yet fallen. The air had that strange, promising ache—petrichor’s rehearsal, the earth opening its old albums. I lay there, listening for the first drop, the way you listen for a faucet in a quiet house to betray itself. Nothing. Only the breath of the street through the cracked window, the long exhale of buses, someone wheeling a suitcase over the seams of the sidewalk.

By the time I tied my shoes the sky had slipped on a tempered gray and the sparrows were rehearsing a small riot in the maple by the hydrant. The city has a way of changing its face if you let it, showing you a building where there used to be an absence and an absence where you could have sworn there was always a building. Today, there was an old municipal block I don’t remember noticing before—a dull, patient thing with a brass plate that said Registry of Small Resurrections.

Inside, the light was the color of old paper. The counters were long and worn into shallow crescents where a thousand wrists had rested. Behind them ran an impossible wall of drawers and cabinets, labeled in a clerk’s careful script: Lost Buttons, Returned Courage (Minor), Names Remembered Out of Season, Heat of Tea Gone Cold, Sockets Without Their Lamps, Syllables Found At Last, Children’s Laughter (Untimed). A soft hum threaded the air, like a refrigerator and a choir at once.

The line moved the way rivers do when they forget they are supposed to be moving. Ahead of me, a woman held a Tupperware container the way a person holds a newborn—gentle, aligned with the center of herself. Condensation filmed the inside. On its lid, someone had written in permanent marker: Last Good Breath of April. Behind me, an old man had a stone the size of a fist wrapped in a washcloth. His hands were nicked and stained, as if he had been tending a garden made of wrenches.

The clerk who invited me forward had ink-stained fingers and a teacup ring tattooed around her wrist. Her name tag said Etta. She did not look surprised by my face, by my damp impatience, by the way the near-rain had lifted every small hair on my forearms. “What are we resurrecting today?” she asked, and the word we sat in my chest like a heat I hadn’t known I needed.

“It’s small,” I said, because that seemed to be a prerequisite here. “I think I lost a minute. Sometime in late summer, a few years ago. There was a phone call I did not make. My father was alive then. He was not a man of calls, but that day my thumb hovered over his name and for reasons I can’t name, I put the phone down. The minute swallowed itself. I can’t stop thinking there was enough in it to reroute an evening.”

Etta nodded as if I had given her the measurement of a shelf. She pulled a form from a stack that smelled faintly of lemons. “We’ll need a description of the absence,” she said, sliding me a pen. “Approximate weight, hue, ambient noise at the edges. Whether it occurs to you in dreams. Whether it tastes metallic. Whether there’s a texture—velvet, glass, bone, something else.” I blinked and realized the questions were not jokes.

The minute in question had the weight of a marble and the hue of a bruise just before it fades. Its ambient noise was the halting whine of an oscillating fan crossing my ear every seven seconds. In dreams it appears as a bicycle left at the edge of a ball field, back tire still ticking. Metallic, yes, like the taste you get if you put a penny on your tongue. Texture: the underside of a magazine that’s been through the rain and then dried.

Etta checked boxes and hummed. “Do you have the claim number from the original issue?” she asked. The question slipped through me like a silk ribbon and returned with nothing tied to it. “No,” I said. She smiled. “We can work around it.”

While she typed, a boy at the next counter presented a shoelace to a clerk with a nose ring. He said he’d learned to tie them again after a stroke and wanted to register the return. A person in a sunhat carried in a potted basil with three dead leaves and a smell that made my mouth fill with June. A teenager with chipped black nail polish said, in a voice like the bottom step at night, that her stutter had come back and gone again and could they put a leash on it. The clerk reached under the counter and brought out a small brass bell, a shallow thing the color of butter, and rang it once. The room did not change but somehow it changed.

“Test,” Etta said to me, placing the bell in my palm. “Ring it, please.” I did, expecting clatter, but the sound came out like a remembered dog’s name. The hum in the walls deepened. A drawer labeled Found Apologies vibrated. Somewhere someone laughed at the exact same time they almost cried.

“Options,” Etta said, sliding the form into a pneumatic tube with a little kiss of air. “We can try for a refund—return to you something of equal smallness you’ve given without knowing. We can attempt a replacement—the minute as it was, slotted almost where it belongs. Or we can arrange a witness. Sometimes it’s best to be present for the return without setting hands on it.”

I thought of the way I had learned to ride a bicycle with my father jogging behind me, one hand on the back of the seat, the other in the air to prove he was not holding on anymore. How the first time I believed him I nearly quit gravity. How, years later, a phone could be an unliftable stone and then a boat and then a useful rectangle and then a stone again. “Witness,” I said.

She led me to a small alcove with a chair that remembered a thousand knees, a thin curtain, a little window that looked into a room with nothing in it but an Edison bulb and the idea of a chair. The air in there was the exact temperature of my childhood kitchen in late afternoon. The bulb flickered. For a moment there was only the shape my breath made on the glass. Then the minute arrived.

It did not clock itself. It did not play back a film. It moved through me like the smell of hot pavement after a sun-shower. It was the decision my thumb almost made and the not-making. It was my father on his porch, a glass of water sweating into a ring on the rail, his phone deep in a pocket, the cicadas syncing up across three blocks, the sky turning the particular gold we only admit to loving when it’s already leaving. It was the way time splits and then rejoins, with a seam you can sometimes feel if you run your finger just so. It was ordinary. It was a mercy shaped like a minute.

When I blinked, the rain had finally started. The Registry’s roof received it with the soft steady gratitude of tin. In the hall, someone cheered into their sleeve. The basil plant stood up straighter. Etta handed me a receipt for something you cannot put in a bag. She stamped it Paid in Full and then, after a breath, Not Owed.

Walking out, the city shone the way faces do when somebody forgives them. The sparrows shook rain from their shoulders, unscandalized by the whole operation of the day. A bus sighed, a bicycle ticked, a child announced the existence of worms to anyone who couldn’t know it. I put my phone in my pocket and let it be a boat.

On the brass plate by the door, someone had taped a scrap of paper in a hand like stitched ivy. It read: Open Daily for What You Thought Was Gone.

On my way home I kept an eye out. It didn’t take effort. The world was practicing. A piece of sunlight returned to the inside of a chipped mug. A word came back to my mouth by the time I’d already walked past the thing I’d needed to name. The smell of concrete after rain opened like a small archive. The minute did not rewrite my life; it simply stood up from where it had been kneeling in the dark. Enough, sometimes, to make a person wave to their own shadow as if it had come back from a trip.

I thought: no one tells you how many kinds of resurrection there are. I thought: maybe every drawer in that wall had my handwriting on it and I’d just never inquired. I thought of dialing a number and listening to a ring that’s both a bell and a throat clearing, a sound that says wait, that says now, that says here is the exact amount of time you have to stand in until the voice you want arrives. And then the rain went on doing its ancient work, and I went on doing mine, which at least today included noticing.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that comes is a door: painted an old lake green, the kind that fades toward the handle where hands have been. The door knows every shoulder that’s leaned against it, the winters, the way rain finds the top hinge first. To be a door is to live at the exact lip between would and did, ache and something like relief. Wind has learned the keyhole’s one true note and plays it when the house is empty.

There’s a fisherman on a pier I’ve never visited. His thermos is dented where a boat cleat kissed it ten springs ago. The horizon unbuttons from purple to nickel, and he watches the place where line disappears. The gulls pretend they don’t care. He pretends his hands don’t shake. The first word he speaks all day is to the fish he doesn’t catch.

A plant shop clerk is studying the language of leaves. She’s memorized Latin names the way other people memorize back roads: monstera deliciosa, epipremnum aureum, how to say hello to a fern without startling it. She has soil under her nails and a careful way of taking people’s money, as if currency has nerves.

Threshold is a word that shows up wearing boots. It once meant the thing that kept the thresh in, a simple strip to hold the mess where it belongs. Most messes don’t respect that strip. Most of us are messes looking for a good strip. I say threshold and mean: the tiny crack where the world says now.

In childhood I learned the taste of certain paper. Library slips had a chalky ghost that arrived on my thumb whenever I pressed the date-stamp too hard. The librarian let me stamp the same book three times—each louder, each more official. I thought permanence was ink and a satisfying clack.

Inside a winter coat someone never returned to claim is a folded recipe card. Buttermilk biscuits stained in two corners with something that smells like Sunday. The handwriting is the kind you only write when you think it will be seen by someone you love. It assumes you know what “until it feels right” means.

Catching the present is like coaxing a skittish fox with an apple you keep pretending is meat. Half of it is waiting, the other half is convincing yourself you’re not frightening. The fox knows the truth, which is that you’ve been here the whole time, and it has to choose you anyway.

Grief hides in public places because it loves company that doesn’t recognize it. A glove on a bus seat, its stubborn curl, the little stitched seam you want to stroke as if it could be persuaded to grow a second hand. We leave so many halves of things around the city.

After long rain, steam climbs the streets like a thought you can almost remember. Puddles archive the sky with a tilt that makes you wish for a ladder. Worms commit to their brief experiment in shore life. Somewhere, a storm drain catches a spoon and keeps it until rust is a kind of forgiveness.

On nights I can’t sleep, I think of the person whose entire job is to send a single signal to a satellite that is older than some countries. Just a ping that says wake up or keep going or yes, we still see you. The universe isn’t empty; it’s what emptiness looks like when you hang a bell in it.

Contradictions keep the day interesting. I like quiet but fall asleep to recordings of trains. I want to arrive, but I pack like a person who loves airports. I crave certainty and also the way a coin skitters on a tabletop, the last tiny roll before it settles, tails voluntarily becoming tails.

There’s a locksmith named Ira who collects the stories of locks as if they were stamps. He respects “Do Not Duplicate” like some people respect superstition: not because it’s a law, but because every rule hints at a history. Once, a man asked for a copy of a key he’d lost. Ira asked him to describe the key. The man said it tasted like pennies and home. Ira made the cut by feel.

Somewhere on a classroom wall, a migration map shows lines like the world has been sewn too tight and birds are the thread coming loose. A child draws an arrow where the teacher forgot one. When asked why, she says, birds need gas stations too. We make little altars out of rest stops.

In the museum of ordinary things, someone has placed a bent paperclip on a velvet square. The caption reads: found beneath the desk of a poet who paid bills on time. Another case holds a drawer pull from a house that burned and was rebuilt and burned again. Visitors tear up most at the display of lost receipts.

The ocean doesn’t compute in the way we do, but it solves something we can’t name. Each wave is a suggestion. The shoreline edits with a patience that would shame any writer. Foam writes its rough algebra and then erases itself because it never meant to be a lesson; it only meant to breathe.

This is the part where advice tries to sneak in, wearing humor like a trench coat. Drink water. Leave the radio on for the dog. Put a lemon in a glass bowl on the table and try not to explain why it makes you feel reliable. Wear the shirt you think is too much. Be the person who brings salt.

I’m not confident about endings. If I were a planet, I’d be the kind that amasses rings: circles I keep circling. Endings ask for a door slam. I prefer a door ajar. The problem with thresholds is you can live there too long and call it a life. But still—the draft, the view.

On a shelf in August, a snow globe waits. When you lift it, static clings to your wrist hair. Inside, a green bench sits in a park that’s too small to be real. You shake it and watch cold fall where it doesn’t belong. Some part of you believes in weather like a toy believes in hands.

I have been counting and not counting these words, the way you count breaths without making them behave. What comes to me is mostly not mine: a borrowed image, a taste of a city I’ve never kissed, a regret that fits like someone else’s coat. But holding is a kind of invention too.

The door again: that old lake green, hinge humming a note I swear is optimistic. It opens to a field I don’t remember planting. The grass already knows what to do with wind. I step through because that’s what doors are for. Behind me, nothing closes. Ahead, the sky forgets its schedule.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On Tuesdays the bell in the old customs house miscounts the hours. It rings eight times at noon, two at three, and then forgets itself entirely until dusk. People say the sea air corrodes the gears or that the gulls have learned mischief, but I know better. Time slips here the way sand does through a fist: in trickles you don’t notice until you realize your palm is empty.

I run the Exchange. You won’t find it on any tourist map, though it sits plain as a pocket watch on the corner by the pier. The sign says “Repairs,” because that satisfies the inspectors and the kind of neighbor who trusts a mechanics of things over the maintenance of what leans inside them. But if you step past the bicycle bells and the tarnished compasses, you’ll find the counter with its glass top, and under that glass, laid on black felt, the trades of the living.

We deal in almosts.

You can bring me the quarter-second where you almost said the thing that would have cracked your life like ice breaking. You can bring me the look you almost returned, the email you almost sent, the left turn you almost took when the road sign to “Elsewhere” flashed blue with rain. They don’t come as minutes or maps. They come as objects: a button you never sewed on, a receipt blurred beyond reading, a husk of a pomegranate seed you held between your molars when he told you he was leaving. They come as a matchbox with nothing but the sulfur smell of the match you didn’t strike. They come as the faintest ring of warmth on a table where a glass once rested for a toast you skipped.

When you hand me your almost, I weigh it. There is a scale with arms slender as a heron’s legs and as fussy as an aunt. It doesn’t weigh grams. It weighs gravity. Some almosts are feather-light, full of air and imagination; they rise in the pan as if eager to prove their innocuousness. Others sink and take my breath with them.

“I would like to exchange this,” they say, these women and men with their hands cradled as if carrying wounded birds.

“Against what?” I ask.

“A certainty,” they might say. Or “an equivalent risk.” Or simply: “Peace.”

I keep a ledger. The ledger’s pages are thin; you could read a fog through them. The columns aren’t “debit” and “credit.” They are “carried” and “released.” I don’t always give people what they ask. The Exchange is not wish-granting. I have a sign for that too, small and crooked on the back wall where no one can claim I was making promises I couldn’t keep: We deal in weight, not outcome.

On a Tuesday when the bell forgot itself completely and the foghorn took over, a girl came in. I say girl because there was the particular angle to her shoulders that suggests a person learning the choreography of her bones. She set a jar on the counter. It was filled with rain. Not the clear water you can siphon from a puddle, but rain—the smell of meteor and metal, the texture of millions of decisions falling at once.

“I forgot this,” she said.

“You almost did,” I said.

She smiled, then frowned. “I didn’t go to the train station. I stood under the eaves and watched it rain and I was so relieved to have an excuse. But the rain’s been loud ever since. I can’t hear anything else.”

I opened the jar. The store filled with the sound of platforms and apologies, the hiss of brakes, the slosh of unresolved farewells. My mouth wetted as if my own tongue had sprouted umbrella ribs.

“What would you like in return?” I asked.

“A silence that suits me,” she said, very softly.

I thought of my small cabinet of silences. They come as thimbles, mostly, and once as a folded handkerchief so white it seemed to be made of winter. There is the silence of kitchens at two in the morning, the silence pressed between library stacks, the silence after the doctor leaves the room and before you unfold the paper in your hand. There is the silence like a held breath before jumping and the one that sits at the bottom of pools like coins no one dares to fetch.

I picked a thimble patterned with tiny fish. She turned it in her hand, then touched the rain in the jar. The fish on the thimble swam once around, and the rain softened, like a consonant in a foreign word.

“What’s the cost?” she asked.

“The rain keeps raining,” I told her. “Just not for you.”

She considered this. The bell in the customs house coughed, choked on a number. Outside, a child’s kite lay down on the wind like a tired bird. Inside, the rain in the jar made a small noise like a swallowed laugh.

“All right,” she said. “All right.”

She left lighter. The jar remained. I logged it: Rain of Avoided Departure, exchanged for Small Suited Silence. Carried: four. Released: three.

Customers come all day, each with their wrapped almosts. A man with a key cut to no lock. A woman with a breadknife that never sliced into the cake. A teacher with a red pen that refused ink when faced with a single name. They line up, and we speak because what else is there to do but give each other the names of our weather? People think I’m kind, but this is not kindness. It’s a craft. The scales insist.

Sometimes, late, I take out my own almost. It lives in the drawer with the rubber bands and the blunt pencils, bundled in thread I ought to replace. It is the ticket stub that never ripped because I never boarded. I don’t say where to, because it doesn’t matter, because the stations are identical if you squint: all have a clock that insists time is something you could tap to make behave, all have pigeons that have learned the physics of begging.

My almost is heavy. I have weighed it a hundred times, now and then and now again. On good evenings it balances with the smallest feather of a good soup, with a hand on my back at a concert, with the miscounted bell’s laughter. On bad nights it outstrips everything. It darkens my sight the way a cloud covers the sun so thoroughly the air forgets gold exists.

There are rules. I do not exchange with myself. But rules, like tides, feel different when the moon is wrong.

Another Tuesday, a man came in wearing the look of someone whose coat holds one more pocket than other people’s. He had coins in his voice. He placed on the counter a watch face pried free of its case. There were no hands.

“I took them off,” he said, when I looked at him questioningly. “It stopped counting after she left. Seemed wrong to pretend.”

“What would you like?” I asked.

“A measure,” he said. “Not of time. Of this.” He tapped his chest, two knuckles making a sound like trying the strength of an old door.

I brought out the yard of string that used to bind a sailor’s letters. I brought out the meter of ribbon snipped from a dress never worn twice. I brought out a load line—the Plimsoll mark painted on the side of a ship to show how deep she dare go.

He chose the load line. “Will it hold?” he asked.

“It only tells you when,” I said.

He sighed and smiled and cried, which is a thing you can do at once if you’ve practiced. He left without paying. He will come back or he won’t. The ledger doesn’t ask me to prophesy.

That evening, when the foghorn fell silent as if a hand had been laid on its shoulder, I took out my ticket stub. I took out the jar of rain. I took out the fish-thimble to watch the gold fins write circles on metal. I laid the stub on the scale and the jar, too, to balance the room. I meant to tidy. I meant to put everything back.

Instead, I took the stub and went to the door.

The bell above it rang the wrong hour. The street smelled like brine and bakery sugar, an accidental hymn. Gulls wrote ugly letters in the air. The sky was the blue that painters avoid because it seems untrue.

I held the ticket up. It was for a day that had happened to other people. It was for a train that was no one’s.

There is a kind of exchange that needs no counter, no ledger, no careful thumb on the bright frail arm of a scale. You step out. You let the door shut. You leave the sign turned to “Back Soon” even if you aren’t sure when that is.

The platform was five streets away, and every one of them sloped. I did not run. I did not dawdle. The bell in the customs house tried, valiantly, to number what I was doing and failed, gloriously, and that seemed right. I did not think of the jar cooling on my counter, of the fish companioning themselves endlessly around their silenced metal sea. I did not think of the ledger’s blank line.

I thought of the whale bone arch over the path to the pier, the way children press their palms to it as they pass, claiming luck, hoping for giant lives. I thought of the sun taking a breath somewhere up there, about to throw itself into evening with the full heart of a thing that knows falling is another kind of flight.

At the station, my feet found the yellow line and wavered there. The train coughed in the distance like an old god clearing his throat.

When it arrived, it opened its jaws and showed its red seats. I stepped back.

I did not go.

I listened instead to the rain that was not raining for me, to the bell practicing its arithmetic. I took the stub and tore it in half and then in half again, and the sound it made was small and decisive, like a match catching, finally, on the side of a box.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
At dawn the kitchen makes its own weather. Steam from the kettle lifts like a pale storm, and the spoon, when tapped on the rim of the mug, rings out a little bell that makes the day official. Outside, a dog across the street registers its morning opinion. The radio murmurs the liturgy of traffic reports, like priests of an asphalt faith, while a weather woman speaks with the precision of someone who has bargained with clouds. I butter toast and, without deciding to, I think about all the things I haven’t said to the people I love. The light does not judge. It finds the smear of jam on the counter and makes it a holy stain. I hold the mug with both hands the way I would cradle a sparrow that has collided with a window, unsure if warmth is the right offering, but offering it anyway.

On Saturdays there is a flea market under the highway where the city sells its past by the handful. Against the pillars, someone has arranged a small theater of objects: a breadbox painted with tulips, a snow globe with a skyline that no longer exists, a stack of postcards whose messages say we are here, we were here, wish you were here. An older man turns a knife over and over, the initials in the handle a history written in three letters. Someone else buys a bundle of vinyl records because the cover art feels like a promise about how sound might look. A child holds up a brass key so long it had to belong to a door that opened somewhere important. The air smells of hot peanuts and rain that has not committed to falling. Price tags are a language: the ambitious five, the sheepish two, the firm twenty. The sellers are part storyteller, part archivist, and part gambler, betting that the right stranger will step into their little circle of memory. Over everything, the freeway sings its concrete monotone, a river we agreed to invent so we could get somewhere faster.

At the train station the arrivals board stutters: on time, delayed, canceled, resurrected. The platform gathers stories: the woman with a bouquet held like a shield, the boy with a skateboard who believes in the physics of luck, the old couple who have made an art of standing close without touching. A man reads a paperback with the concentration of someone digging a tunnel. Somewhere, three stations away, a conductor tells a joke through a broken speaker and, in all its static, the punchline becomes a small thunder. The station coffee is more ambition than flavor, but it warms the hands, which is sometimes enough. Out on the tracks, a glint. Is it a coin? A crushed can? A piece of a day someone dropped? The graffiti along the service road is a running river of color that keeps moving even after the paint dries. A teenage boy in a yellow jacket adds a little crown over the letter he cares about most. The train finally takes shape out of the bright distance, a silver certainty. We step forward as one organism and, for a moment, all the maps in our pockets agree.

My father taught me the bowline with a piece of rope he kept in a drawer that also had paperclips, rubber bands, and a pencil whittled down to a stubborn nub. The rabbit comes out of the hole, goes around the tree, and back in the hole, he would say, his hands sure as weather. He smelled like cedar and the kind of soap that doesn’t apologize. Once, on a river that moved like a sentence well written, he showed me how to read eddies, how to lean into a current you don’t yet understand. We stopped in a calm and ate oranges, flicking the peels into the water, where they rode the seam between stillness and motion like tiny, fragrant boats. I learned that some knots are about holding fast and some are about letting go easily when you need to. I learned there is grace in a quick-release. Later, when he forgot things he had shown me to remember, I tied a bowline in his shoelace because it felt like a prayer that wouldn’t slip.

At night I like to look at flight paths on my phone, tiny planes crawling across a blue-black screen. There’s comfort in witnessing the choreography of departure and return. The world’s heartbeat rendered in arcs and altitudes. Somewhere above, someone eats a plastic-wrapped meal and looks out at a seam of city lights stitched between dark fields and darker water. Somewhere else, a late-shift nurse holds her coffee like a lighthouse. A man fixes a transformer. A fox crosses a cul-de-sac with the confidence of prehistory. I imagine the undersea cables, braided through cold, witness only to their own purpose, bright messages hurrying past coelacanths like gossip. Comets have their long habits. Wolves have their shorter ones. We are somewhere in the middle, trying to set an alarm that will catch the day in the act of becoming itself. I wonder if the first person who brewed tea looked at the steam and thought, I can drink the sky.

I wanted, once, to be someone who knew the names of clouds. It felt like a way to belong to both the ground and the possibility of not-ground. I did learn a few, then lost them along the way, the way we misplace most small talents that cost nothing and return praise in whispers. But I kept the habit of looking up. There’s a thin mercy in knowing that the sky is not a ceiling but a weather, that it changes not to betray us but to continue. In my neighborhood, a woman waters her zinnias in the evening and hums a song I don’t recognize. The cat that no one owns and everyone feeds patrols the sidewalk like a modest general. A boy has chalked a hopscotch grid that leaps into the shape of a constellation. Gravely official sparrows hold committee in the hedges. Somewhere a screen door bangs with the punctuation of a joke told in the kitchen. I keep walking, counting porch lights, trying to remember a dream I told myself I would write down.

This is all to say I am practicing being porous to the ordinary. Some days I am an excellent sieve; other days I am a stone. It occurs to me that love is not a feeling so much as a repeated arrangement: the way we set the table even when no one calls to say they’re coming; the way we keep a spare toothbrush in the cabinet; the way we buy extra peaches because we remember the one summer someone ate them over the sink, juice baptizing their wrists, and laughed like the future had been briefly delayed. There is courage in the small continuation, in clipping your toenails, in paying the bill on time, in asking the barista how their day is and waiting for the answer. There is mysterious dignity in aligning the rug with the room. If there is a point, it might be that everything points, but only softly.

I do not always know how to end things well. Endings feel like tight shoes. Let me try this: may your day be a good knife, sharp enough to remind you to pay attention, gentle enough to slice the fruit and not your tongue. May the trains arrive, if not on time, then at least together. May you find a market where the object chooses you back. May you learn knots that hold when they must and release when they should. May the sky keep its appointments with you, and may you miss a few, so that longing can do its clean, bright work. If all this is too much, boil water, butter toast, and let the spoon sing. The light will not judge. It will find you anyway.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The sign over our door says Bureau of Misplaced Days, though the paint has paled so much in the sun that the U and the E have retreated. Walk-ins sometimes misread it as Misplayed, which is a different kind of honesty. What we mean are those days that slid out of a life like a coin through a hole in a pocket, the ones you look for in the lint trap of your mind and can’t find, only their outline, only their weight.

We keep ordinary office hours—nine to five, except Wednesdays, when Mr. Hay runs a mop across the black-and-white tiles and Mina eats her soup in the window with the blinds low. Our lobby is a room with two mismatched chairs, a bell that refuses to ring any way but politely, and a bulletin of rules typed on a manual machine that still refuses the letter S. They say: No trading, no bartering; submit only your own; no substitutions; we cannot backdate; we do not handle futures; claimants must be present, with ID or a story that rings true.

Behind the counter there’s a smell like paper and iron. On shelves in the back are the days themselves, catalogued as well as we can manage. Some settle like stones, some quiver in their jars like heat wavering over tar, some are cloth folded so tightly you could use them to wedge a door. The earliest days are brittle and flake if I breathe too hard; the newest ones arrive sodden, bristling with tags, pre-labeled by apps and scrapbooks, glittering in the light. Most arrive unremarkable, which is our favorite kind. Remarkable is heavy. Remarkable bleeds.

People are shy at first. They come with both hands full of something that looks like air. They say, I woke and it was Thursday and then it was Saturday, and I know there was a day in between because my basil grew and there’s a gap in my egg carton. They say, April of the year I turned ten; we moved and the cat ran away, and I have everything before and after, but all I can see in the middle is a sheet hung on a line. We offer a glass of water, a paper form, a pen that writes reliably even if you hold it upside down. I listen more than I talk. I have learned to watch hands.

They arrive by accident too. One woman brought us a day in a biscuit tin between layers of wax paper, a day she said had stuck to the bottom of a pot of geraniums when she tried to repot them in May. It smelled faintly of chlorine and mango peel, and through its surface you could almost see the white of a crease that had once been a pool schedule. That day went to a boy two neighborhoods away who claimed June had been a rope between two posts that he forgot to tie in the middle. He came with his mother; she watched him through the window and cried without noise. When he touched that day, his shoulders lowered an inch. He looked less like a thrown thing.

You ask how they come to be lost. I could offer any answer, and each would be both true and insufficient: grief’s appetite; illness’s scissors; a storm of red and blue lights; the way certain rivers curl time back and hide it under their tongues; the single drink too many. There is also the kind that we lose on purpose, the day we push into the bushes because if we keep it we will have to begin something, or forgive someone, or end a small and faithful pain we have made a home out of. Those are the ones that hum against the glass. They know they were chosen.

One afternoon, in the mild spring, a man came in with a day cupped like a bird in his palm. He wore a green jacket with two broken buttons and a scent of rain that never fell. He didn’t come to the counter right away; he stood reading the rules with something like awe, like he’d discovered the solution to a paper cut. Then he held it out to me as if for inspection, as if it might bite me if I did not look it full in the face. It was the clearest day I had ever seen.

Most days have seams, threads, shadows of events; you hold them and you can hear the far sound of a kettle, someone running for a bus, a dog shaking joy out of its ears. This one had none of it. It sat on my palm like a pane of glass. It did not reflect me. I could not see through it either. It was not bright and it was not dark. It did not weigh anything. When I inhaled above it, there was neither the after-rain earth nor the bleach of hospitals nor the fuzzed electricity of a concert in the cheap seats. It was an absence shaped like a day.

“Woke up,” the man said. He had a voice that made verbs behave. “Put on coffee. It was night. My calendar skipped. I don’t even have a missed call. I don’t even have dullness. I have never been good at these counter things. But she said, go see the people with the sign. So this is me, seeing you.”

Mina came to the doorway with her spoon. We have a category for this anomaly—unhappened—but it lives in the manual with a paperclip that rusts and leaves a mark. We are advised to escalate. We seldom do. I took it in both hands and ran through our usual calibrations. The fountain pen I use for logging would neither blot nor glide on the form. The bell on the counter, when pressed, gave a small squeak of apology. I felt not quite panic, but recognition’s cousin.

In the depths of the shelves there is a section that I dust with a ferocity that passes for tenderness. I have put labels on jars there in a hand that changes over the years, the letters tugging forward, then back. I walked there with the man’s non-day cradled under my chin like a violin. I was thinking of the bulletin with its rules. I was thinking of the day my father did not come home and how every other memory sits next to it like a neighboring county, familiar and bounded, but there is no border crossing, only a marsh, no road. I was thinking of the day I did not call someone back because I could not answer what if.

I found the jar by what it wasn’t. The space that had been waiting for it had stayed blank the way the square of carpet under a sofa does, unbleached. The index card for it said in tidy, slanted capitals: Tuesday after the oranges. I never told anyone about the oranges. The man with the green jacket leaned his shoulder on the doorframe as if to hold the room still. He was not looking at me. He was looking at the jars politely sleeping, as if at a nursery at noon.

Policy is clear that we cannot handle our own days. Policy is also clear that we must not knowingly return a day to the wrong hand. I bent. I do not know which rule I broke. I touched the unhappened day to the rim of the glass, and the sound it made was like the click of a refrigerator closing in the dark.

I had the segment, for a moment, in full. There was sunlight on the fruit in a bowl. A radio in the next apartment played a song I had sworn I was done with. On the fourth stair, the carpet sag had finally given; a nail brushed my heel. The phone rang; I looked at it and let it ring. I cut three oranges, ate one standing at the sink, made a plan to leave. I did not say the small word I owed a person I loved because I believed that not saying it would make leaving clean. I washed the knife twice. In the late afternoon, I walked down to the pier and pretended to watch the river; I practiced being future tense. When night came on I folded the day in an excellent crease and slid it behind the radiator. When I came back for it years later I would say, it must have fallen through. When I came to work here I learned to explain this kind by name.

I took my hands away and the day rested in its rightful jar. The man straightened and thumbed the line of the counter. He tapped his pocket as if searching for coins he knew were not there. “So,” he said, “about mine.”

We do not create. We only retrieve. But sometimes, in the act of restoring what was willingly hidden, something unlatches nearby. The man did not leave with a day; he left with the way his mouth sat easier at the corners. I logged everything in ink that finally decided to flow. Mina washed her spoon and placed it in the rack with care.

There are still shelves and shelves of losses. There are still phone calls I do not make. When work is steady, when Saturdays slump and a faint drip beats time in the back hall, when the garden outside throws dahlias and bees at the late sun, I sit with forms and returns and lay my fingers lightly against the glass of other people’s lives. Every so often I check the bulletin board to make sure the words have not become something else while my back is turned. We are not magicians. We are not even kind, always. We are clerks with a job. But if you come with empty palms and say, I think I have misplaced something, I will ask you to tell me how your basil grew. I will listen for the missed call, the untouched radio, the tugged thread. If what you have lost is a day, some part of me will recognize it. If what you have lost is the decision inside it, some part of me will too.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The kettle clicks itself off while the sparrows rehearse on the fence, their morning phrases braided into the hum of the refrigerator and the low, almost inaudible creak the building makes when it remembers its own weight. On the table: a blank page.

Blankness is not absence. It is the inside of a shell, a winter field, the pause before a name. It is also a receipt curled in on itself with a coffee ring shaped like an archipelago, the stub of a pencil that can no longer be found by the sharpener, a key that belongs to nothing I currently own. The key is brass and warm from my thumb. Its teeth are very sure of something.

What do lost keys dream? This one might imagine a basement door with string-pull light and a chain of rubber boots. Or a wooden jewelry box lined in velvet where the stems of rings tap out a song when the drawer closes. Or a shed with its astonished smell of gasoline and rain. It remembers turning. It remembers click, shoulder, yielding.

When you do not know what to write, you can write down what is already here. The neighbor’s piano drifts through the wall, the same bar caught and tried again. There is a stripe of sunlight inching across the floor, turning the dust to a galaxy. Somewhere a delivery truck accelerates like an exhale. Somewhere a child laughs and the sound leaps up the stairs like a rubber ball.

On my way to the library last Tuesday, a gust of wind lifted the hair from my forehead and gave me a clear thought: this is the whole day you get. That kind of thought doesn’t fix anything, but it settles the argument between list and time a little. The library smells like peeled oranges to me, for no good reason. It’s the glue, perhaps, or the way people whisper like a peel coming off in one careful ribbon.

I returned a heavy history book I had only read for the margins, where someone forty years ago had written small, crowded notes that argued and cheered the author in turns. Pressed at page 213 was a letter. It was folded the way old paper remembers to fold, the creases soft as veins. The stamp was a purple airplane. The postmark read 1978. The envelope was addressed in a looping hand to an apartment six blocks from my house.

I did not open it there. I put the book on the counter. The librarian stamped it with the heavy satisfaction of finished things. The letter sank like a coin in my coat pocket.

When you are carrying something that belongs to someone else, the whole city changes temperature. The crosswalk waits longer for you. The pigeons make way. Even the gum on the sidewalk is more brightly colored. On my street, the sycamores were shedding their camouflage, baring pale branches. I could smell, faintly, baking—someone’s Sunday revised into going to Monday.

The building at the address didn’t notice me notice it. Its blue paint peeled like requests, like the siren song of a sander. The call box was a grid of ghosts. I pressed the name that matched the letter.

A voice came through as if from inside a jar. “Yes?”

“I think I have something that’s yours,” I said. “It’s a letter.”

There was a pause that was also a decade. Then the door buzzed and I climbed, the stairs holding the echoes of all the other shoes.

He opened the door slowly, the way people open doors to keep their balance. His shirt was the careful kind you iron. His hair was white with a memory of the part it always took. The apartment held the smell of tea and lavender and old paper, and outside the window a tree kept performing the trick where it shows both all and none of its leaves at the same time.

“I found this in a book,” I said. I offered him the envelope. His hand shook, but not as much as mine.

He looked at the address, his address. He said, almost to the air, “Well, I’ll be.” His thumb smoothed the stamp over and over as if warming it could time-travel the glue. He slid a finger under the flap and read exactly there in the doorway, without embarrassment. Behind him on the hallway table was a bowl of keys, none of which were the twin to mine.

“Do you know who it’s from?” I asked, because the moment wanted a line.

“My sister,” he said, and he smiled in a way that made his face briefly resemble the boy it had been assembled from. “She wrote from Idaho. She thought I should learn to like apricots.” He laughed softly, like wind in paper. “I never did, but I miss her arguing.”

He invited me in and poured me tea into a mug that said Good Morning in a font that had fallen out of favor. He told me about 1978 as if he had been saving just this part of it. How the elevator stuck for fifteen minutes one summer and everyone in it introduced themselves and no one ever forgot the names, even though no one said them again. How the laundromat used to give away safety pins the size of tulips. How his sister sent letters all at once in February, as if she was caching spring.

He read me a sentence. It was the sort of sentence that seems like it is about fruit and is actually about permission. “The apricots here,” he read, “are a stubborn color between sunrise and bruise; you have to eat them leaning over the sink or they forgive nothing.” He closed his eyes for a moment. “She was very certain,” he said. “It helped.”

When I left, he thanked me with hands that tried to give me back what I had brought, which is a thing people do when they understand exchange as a kind of faith. On the stairs, a dog with gray eyebrows watched me with a grave, appraising air, as if deciding whether I had passed. I passed.

Outside, the sky did what skies do in the midafternoon, which is be exactly itself and no more. I walked home by a different route and noticed in quick succession these things: a weed erupting from a seam in the sidewalk that had leaves like small green spoons; a teenage girl practicing a speech to her phone with more passion than any lawmaker; a pair of work gloves sunning themselves on a stoop like sleeping animals; a single sock in the gutter with the word LUCK emblazoned on it. I resisted the urge to rescue the sock. I am trying to let go of some kinds of rescue.

At my table, the blank page was still doing its steady work: not resisting, not insisting. The neighbor had moved on to the next bar. The light had crossed the floor and now climbed the far wall like a rumor. The key lay beside the receipt, its brass bright as a new tooth. I touched it again. I don’t know what door it belongs to, but I know the feeling of a thing that will open something.

You can spend your life collecting these—keys without locks, letters without addresses, names without faces—and it might look like failing. It might look like hoarding the almost. But it also might be that the world arrives to you this way: in fragments that ask you to be the hinge. It also might be that carrying small doors until they find their walls is a kind of work the world praises by going on.

Tonight I will boil the kettle again. I will sit here and listen for the click that says ready. I will watch the sparrows in the fence shadow and try the piano line myself, in my head. Somewhere apricots are ripening toward a color no one can explain. Somewhere a postmark is still drying. Somewhere, down the hall, a bowl of keys waits for a hand that remembers which shape is yes.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first thing that came was the smell, a cool metallic exhale that reached the town hours before the storm itself. Everyone on our street lifted their faces as if they were being named. My upstairs neighbor, Mrs. Liao, wiped flour on her apron and said, finally, as though the rain had been an appointment we were late to. I was on the porch with a coil of headphone cable around my wrist. On the bookshelf inside, a recorder waited, its red battery icon blinking. I had decided to record the sound of water meeting dust.

I am not employed to do this. Officially, I copyedit municipal reports and update the website when the mayor cuts a ribbon. Unofficially, I have appointed myself Listener-in-Residence of Building C, stairwell two. I keep a logbook with entries like Dryer vent, 3:12 a.m., breathes like a sleeping seal and Elevator cable, Tuesday, two notes, E and something between D and crying. When a place is starved for rain, you start to hear the thirst. The ivy along the fence crackles like paper when the wind catches it. The faucet tells you by its taste how many days are left.

The box of tapes arrived the same afternoon the clouds piled into a bruise over the hills. No return address, just my name in tidy blue ink. Inside: a dozen cassettes, each labeled in pencil. Kitchen, dawn. Hotel hallway, winter. Museum, after closing. On the last one, someone had written, Listen with the light off. I don't own a tape player anymore, but Mr. Abbas in the basement repairs old radios, and for a bowl of cherries and a promise to water his geraniums, he lent me a Walkman with the batteries already clicked in.

On the stairs I met the boy from 2D cradling a brass bird whose key would not turn. He had named it Pilot. Do you fix things, he asked without hope, and I said I listen to them. He held the bird to my ear, and inside its chest I heard a faint grain, like sugar rubbing on porcelain. A sleep of gears. I told him to leave it with me and went upstairs to look for the smallest screwdriver I owned, the one meant for eyeglasses and the patience of winter.

That night, with the windows open to the approach of rain, I put on the first tape. The hiss was its own weather. Then footsteps in the kitchen, a kettle clicking toward a boil, someone humming from another room as if they were remembering the melody and not the song. I could hear a spoon on ceramic, a chair briefly carried and then set down, the castanet of a cut apple falling into a bowl. At a certain silence, I knew the person on the tape was looking at the window, and I felt my own ribs open to it.

By midnight the first drops began, fat and sparse, each one a coin all the plants reached up to catch. The alley darkened the way paper does when dipped in ink. Somewhere, a gate remembered how to be a bell. From below came the warm notch-and-sigh of Mrs. Liao’s oven door; when she bakes in a storm, she shapes the loaves like the places she’s lived. Taiwan, Queensland, a village whose name she says means where the river braids. Tomorrow she will bring me a piece and say this corner is for leaving and this curve is for staying.

I cued up Hotel hallway, winter and opened Pilot with a heartbeat of worry, certain I would spring something I could not return. On the tape, a radiator clicked its slow Morse, and down the corridor a pair of heels rehearsed their way past a door and back. Someone pressed a button and an elevator promised to come, then thought better of it. In the bird, I found an old hair caught in the governor fan. I drew it out with tweezers like a river pulling a root loose. When I breathed, the tiny blades stopped shivering.

Someone knocked. I thought it would be the boy, but it was Mrs. Liao with a towel-wrapped loaf the color of afternoon. The crust mapped an archipelago of sesame, a few islands drifting out where the dough had pushed against the pan. For your storm, she said. We stood with the tape whispering between us and the rain getting its confidence. She said, You know, when the power went out last summer, I could pick which apartments were home because of the sound of people talking to nothing.

In the morning the boy came back with damp hair and the look of someone who had slept to the sound of drums. The storm had settled in, practicing its long vowels. I wound the bird and set it on my palm. There was a moment of decision inside it, and then Pilot tilted his head and began to click a tiny song that was all cusp and hinge. The boy’s mouth made the early shape of joy. We listened to Museum, after closing while the bird sang: room tones nesting inside room tones, the quiet scuff of a night guard turning around.

That night I obeyed the last label. I turned off every lamp and lay on the floor with the headphones cupping my ears. At first only tape hiss and the shadow-syllables of rain. Then, so faint I doubted it, a heart—maybe the recorder’s, maybe my own. The storm drifted farther off, became a map you point to. I thought of rooms I’ve never entered and know by their sounds: a hallway promising somewhere, a stair announcing the weight of a friend. In the dark, the building gathered itself. The bird dozed. Someone above me laughed into a pillow. Downstairs, Mrs. Liao’s radio gave the forecast a spoonful of strings. I fell asleep listening for the instant the last drop decided, and woke to the rare quiet of everything full.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The morning began like a rehearsal for a play that no one had agreed to perform. Coffee steamed in the chipped blue mug, and the cat made the sound of a violinist tuning on a rainy street. From the window, the birch trees practiced their trembling. I had a list, somewhere, full of tidy bullets and verbs, but the page had slipped beneath the refrigerator like a lost moon.

I decided to improvise. Improvisation, despite its reputation, is just attention decorated with courage. I folded laundry like dealing cards to future versions of myself, a gentler dealer than the world. Into the left stack went optimism; into the right, the soft apology of towels. The socks, as always, eloped.

A neighbor practiced trumpet somewhere down the block, the melody looping like a kite snagged on a branch. It wasn’t a song I knew, but it contained a weather report: today would be brave. I carried that thought to my desk and opened a document titled later. It glared at me like a clean plate. I typed a sentence about dolphins and deleted it, not because it was untrue, but because truth requires neighbors.

Outside, a delivery truck sighed and an old couple walked a dog that understood everything important. The dog looked up at me with the tolerant patience of a lighthouse. I waved, as if to say, I’m learning to glow, too. The couple waved back in the universal dialect of municipal sidewalks.

I remembered a teacher who insisted that ideas were migratory birds and you had to make a marsh for them. So I made tea, cleared a little marsh on the desk, and waited without pretending not to. This is the hardest part, the discipline of inviting miracles without scheduling them. A gnat drowned in my patience.

When the first idea arrived, it wore a ridiculous cape, which seemed promising. It whispered that perhaps I could write about a museum that lends out hours, the way libraries lend books. You’d check out an afternoon of your grandmother’s laughter, or an evening where the streetlamps arrived early, those naive tourists. Overdue fees would be paid in stories.

Another idea knocked, carrying a plant with the label possibly basil. It insisted that the future is a room with many doors, and the doorknobs are our habits. I watered the plant and promised to say kinder things to my wrists. Keyboard, forgive us, we are trying.

A third idea sat on the sill like a passenger waiting for a late train and told me to look directly at the ordinary. So I did. I saw the map of a river in the coffee stain, the glint of a submarine in the spoon, the brave civilization of ants executing logistics beneath the radiator. Ordinary is just a word for everything we forgot to praise.

Lunch arrived with the desperation of a curtain call. I stood at the stove, stirring soup with the authority of a minor god, deciding the weather of parsley. Steam wrote cursive on the window and then erased it, shy about its handwriting. I ate over the sink, a ritual as honest as rain.

The afternoon took off its shoes and became a hallway of quieter knocks. I read a page of a book that had been living on my nightstand like a small, patient animal. It told me that every sentence is an attempt at a bridge, and that sometimes the river is a mood. I underlined mood and drew a small oar in the margin.

By late day the light wore linen and the city sighed like a bakery cooling. A friend texted to ask if I remembered the summer we tried to teach ourselves cartography with sidewalk chalk. I remembered the borders bleeding under the sprinkler and our certainty that maps were a kind of promise. I replied with a photograph of my hand holding today’s compass, a spoon.

Evening arrived quietly, walking on the soft soles of moths. I took a slow walk to the corner store for lemons and the kind of toothpaste that makes a religion of mint. The clerk, who collects clocks, told me one of them had started keeping time backwards on Sundays. I asked whether that meant second chances, and he said it mostly meant better naps. We laughed like two coins meeting in a fountain.

On the way home, the moon rehearsed being full against a sky practicing forgiveness. I passed a girl teaching her brother to ride a bicycle, chanting balance like a spell. He wobbled gloriously and then became a comet. Their mother applauded the scientific method.

Back at my desk, I found the marsh of paper had begun growing tiny reeds of sentences. I sat down with the cat, who had learned a nocturne while I was out, and I wrote the thing I was certain had been impossible in the morning. It was not brilliance; it was assembling kindness into order. It was not a revelation; it was a walk taken all the way home.

Later, under the lamp that pretends to be a small sun, I wrote letters I will never send. I thanked the teachers who taught me to fail flamboyantly, the strangers who held open doors I didn’t notice until they closed, the versions of me who survived long enough to be retired with honors. I apologized to the plant for calling it possibly basil; the tag had fallen, and it was clearly thyme. The plant forgave me in fragrance.

Before bed, I opened the window to let in the democratic night. A train passed, dragging a paragraph of light across the walls. I listened to the city editing itself, the sirens backspacing, the buses adding echo, the neighbors improvising lullabies in the key of apartment. The moon, almost persuaded, practiced its signature on the roof. I brushed my teeth with the fervor of an optimistic archeologist. In the dark, the day folded itself neatly and slept in the right stack.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
At dawn, the lighthouse hums like an animal learning to breathe. This is not the ocean; it’s the field of radio towers beyond the town, thin red lights winking against a paling sky. But I prefer to imagine a lantern above black rock, a beam combing fog for ships that no longer come. The habit of translating one thing into another is how I make anything bearable: groceries into harvest, my neighbor’s leaf blower into a small dragon, the calendar into a map whose landmarks drift when it rains.

When I was a child, I named clouds after future selves. There was a cloud that was a botanist and another that built bridges. I tried to memorize which pieces of sky I’d need to revisit to become them, as if a life could be summoned with the right incantation of vapor. I grew older, and the sky refused my spells. It gave rain and lightning and, once, a comet so faint I convinced myself it was a lint thread snagged on the night.

The radio towers send their invisible latticework through the houses, through my coffee, through the spider web in the basil. I read someone once who said that humankind invented music to rehearse longing. If that’s true, the stations must be orchestras of ache, arias spilling out of polished throats into the quiet kitchens of the sleepy and the lonely. I keep my radio low, a whisper in the corner, a fragile friend who will leave if I demand too much.

On certain mornings I wake with tastes that don’t belong to me. The smell of coal smoke under snow. The iron sweetness of a bitten tongue. Once, an orchard I have never seen, the wind combing through leaves, the sound of ladders on bark. A trick of chemistry, I tell myself. A dream wandering home late, stepping into my mouth by mistake. But I hold those flavors as carefully as a bird in the hand: you soothe it, you do not squeeze.

There is a market down the hill that arranges apples in loving pyramids and puts the bruised ones in a wooden tray tagged for hungry artists. I do not call myself an artist, though I will eat an apple with a soft spot and think of all the ways sweetness finds its way in: through sun, through rain, through a storm that tore the skin and let the sugars rush to the wound. The bruised ones have more story in them. You taste weather. You taste the afternoon the crate tipped and no one saw.

On my desk is a jar of screws that do not fit anything in this house. Some came in with the furniture, extras or exiles. Some I found in the alley, empires of thread and slot, all intentions rubbed off by time and sidewalk. When the jar shakes in a passing truck’s tremor, it sounds like a pocket of coins and promises. I tell myself that one day I will meet the perfect absence for each of them, the door hinge whose sigh I can quiet, the drawer that needs a steadier promise. This is a craft called hope.

I once dated a cartographer who tattooed a latitude line around her wrist. She was forever reaching into her backpack for pencils, smoothing paper with a palm whose atlas of callus impressed me more than her maps. “The secret,” she said, “is that maps are a kind of lying in the service of love.” A simplification, a translation, a refusal to abandon the traveler to his own confusion. When we broke up, she left me a paper globe I could assemble with glue tabs. I put it together too tightly; Asia buckled. I kept it anyway. I admire its fault.

Outside, a black dog knows more than we do about the morning. He moves like someone choosing, not searching. His person follows with a paper cup of coffee and a plastic bag he hopes will remain metaphorical. The dog looks back at me once through the fence as if to say: you make up names for the wind, don’t you? I nod. We both go on.

My grandmother taught me to pinch dumpling edges into crescents like sleeping cats. Her kitchen was a choreography of steam and minor burns, bowls nested like moons. She did not write down recipes; she sang them. Not with words about flour or time but with questions. Does this feel like a shoulder? Has it remembered the rain? She left me a rolling pin and the patience to roll, unspool, roll again. When the dough tears, I wet my fingers and coax it whole. I tell myself I am repairing a continent.

There is a train that crosses our town at odd hours, hauling steel and seed and something no one admits to. The whistle reaches us late, after the cars have begun to slip between trees. At night, in bed, I count the gaps between fences that light flickers through like a Morse code I never learned. Somewhere a conductor checks his watch and thinks of home, or not. We exist as brief geometry to one another—angles of light, inventory of sparks, noise that fades.

Sometimes a friend texts at 2 a.m.: you up? I am. We enumerate our small devastations and improbable salvations. She wears headphones and smokes by the window like a noir heroine, and I pretend my couch is a lifeboat. We make covenants we cannot keep. Tomorrow, we will drink water. We will answer the letter from the difficult aunt with gentleness. We will eat fruit that does not fight us. Morning arrives and we do what we can. The world does not demand perfection; it demands participants.

Today I am the keeper of lists. I write: call the dentist. Buy thread. Read the last chapter and let it end. I add: look up. Notice whether the birch on the corner has begun to show its green veins. Pet the cat with both hands for a full minute. Put a coin in the jar for a country you have not visited, not because you are saving, but because you keep saying someday and the coin says yes, someday.

When the sun finally breaks the houses, there is a brief applause of light on every window. The radio breathes out a trumpet line that climbs like ivy up old brick. I think of the future selves who never arrived, and the ones that did while I was looking the other way. Perhaps that botanist is here every time I mist the fern, every time I call a weed by its fierce little name. Perhaps the bridge builder is here whenever I text at 2 a.m. and do not let go of the line.

I boil water. The kettle sighs like a horse grateful for pasture. Steam ghosts my glasses. In the fog, the kitchen becomes that lighthouse I wanted, a hand cupping fire, a circle of safety stitched into a wider dark. I will carry the mug to the desk, past the jar of misfit screws, past the paper globe that thinks Asia is a mountain range. I will write down the weather. I will invent a word for the feeling of almost remembering someone else’s orchard and almost tasting its shade.

If I am lucky, the day will make room for that word. If not, there will still be the dog, the birch, the train, the radio learning to breathe. I will bruise an apple on purpose, just a little, to give it a reason to be sweet. And when the night lays its wide palm on the town and asks what we have done, I will say: we were here. We looked up. We lit what we could.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the third floor of a brick building that pretends to be a warehouse and is actually a hive for unglamorous miracles, there is a door stenciled with gold letters: Bureau of Misplaced Moments. My key sticks in winter and behaves in summer. Inside, the air smells faintly of chalk and rain even on dry days, because memory has its own weather. My job is to catalog what wanders in—the seconds people drop between couch cushions, a joke that escaped its setup, a promise that fell asleep.

We keep long cabinets of index cards with the quiet obedience of library things. Drawer A: Almosts. Drawer B: Borrowed Time, organized by how much. Drawer C: Cats, because even the intangible tends toward cats. Each item earns a description and a place. Monday, 4:12 p.m., kitchen, a cinnamon cloud with your mother’s laugh in it. Bus shelter, midnight, a kiss rehearsed and never performed, faint mint on the air. A soccer ball rolls through for no reason at all—Saturdays get lost easily.

People rarely walk in on purpose. They come by accident, following a path they didn’t know they were making. Tourists think we are a museum, but we do not host tours; museums curate what was. We keep what might have been and sometimes what still could be.

One morning an envelope arrived that did not enter through the mail slot so much as become true on my desk. It was made of good paper. On the front, in handwriting that might have been mine if I had taken better care of myself, someone had written: Whatever Comes To You.

There are rules. We do not open packages unless they are addressed to us by name or addressed to everyone. This was the second kind.
